\### 代码文件: docs\\internals\\protocol.rst

```rst

.. \_message-protocol:



===================

&#x20;Message Protocol

===================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:



.. \_message-protocol-task:

.. \_internals-task-message-protocol:



Task messages

=============



.. \_message-protocol-task-v2:



Version 2

\---------



Definition

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   properties = {

&#x20;       'correlation\_id': uuid task\_id,

&#x20;       'content\_type': string mimetype,

&#x20;       'content\_encoding': string encoding,



&#x20;       # optional

&#x20;       'reply\_to': string queue\_or\_url,

&#x20;   }

&#x20;   headers = {

&#x20;       'lang': string 'py'

&#x20;       'task': string task,

&#x20;       'id': uuid task\_id,

&#x20;       'root\_id': uuid root\_id,

&#x20;       'parent\_id': uuid parent\_id,

&#x20;       'group': uuid group\_id,



&#x20;       # optional

&#x20;       'meth': string method\_name,

&#x20;       'shadow': string alias\_name,

&#x20;       'eta': iso8601 ETA,

&#x20;       'expires': iso8601 expires,

&#x20;       'retries': int retries,

&#x20;       'timelimit': (soft, hard),

&#x20;       'argsrepr': str repr(args),

&#x20;       'kwargsrepr': str repr(kwargs),

&#x20;       'origin': str nodename,

&#x20;       'replaced\_task\_nesting': int

&#x20;   }



&#x20;   body = (

&#x20;       object\[] args,

&#x20;       Mapping kwargs,

&#x20;       Mapping embed {

&#x20;           'callbacks': Signature\[] callbacks,

&#x20;           'errbacks': Signature\[] errbacks,

&#x20;           'chain': Signature\[] chain,

&#x20;           'chord': Signature chord\_callback,

&#x20;       }

&#x20;   )



Example

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



This example sends a task message using version 2 of the protocol:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   # chain: add(add(add(2, 2), 4), 8) == 2 + 2 + 4 + 8



&#x20;   import json

&#x20;   import os

&#x20;   import socket



&#x20;   task\_id = uuid()

&#x20;   args = (2, 2)

&#x20;   kwargs = {}

&#x20;   basic\_publish(

&#x20;       message=json.dumps((args, kwargs, None)),

&#x20;       application\_headers={

&#x20;           'lang': 'py',

&#x20;           'task': 'proj.tasks.add',

&#x20;           'argsrepr': repr(args),

&#x20;           'kwargsrepr': repr(kwargs),

&#x20;           'origin': '@'.join(\[os.getpid(), socket.gethostname()])

&#x20;       }

&#x20;       properties={

&#x20;           'correlation\_id': task\_id,

&#x20;           'content\_type': 'application/json',

&#x20;           'content\_encoding': 'utf-8',

&#x20;       }

&#x20;   )



Changes from version 1

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



\- Protocol version detected by the presence of a ``task`` message header.



\- Support for multiple languages via the ``lang`` header.



&#x20;   Worker may redirect the message to a worker that supports

&#x20;   the language.



\- Meta-data moved to headers.



&#x20;   This means that workers/intermediates can inspect the message

&#x20;   and make decisions based on the headers without decoding

&#x20;   the payload (that may be language specific, for example serialized by the

&#x20;   Python specific pickle serializer).



\- Always UTC



&#x20;   There's no ``utc`` flag anymore, so any time information missing timezone

&#x20;   will be expected to be in UTC time.



\- Body is only for language specific data.



&#x20;   - Python stores args/kwargs and embedded signatures in body.



&#x20;   - If a message uses raw encoding then the raw data

&#x20;     will be passed as a single argument to the function.



&#x20;   - Java/C, etc. can use a Thrift/protobuf document as the body



\- ``origin`` is the name of the node sending the task.



\- Dispatches to actor based on ``task``, ``meth`` headers



&#x20;   ``meth`` is unused by Python, but may be used in the future

&#x20;   to specify class+method pairs.



\- Chain gains a dedicated field.



&#x20;   Reducing the chain into a recursive ``callbacks`` argument

&#x20;   causes problems when the recursion limit is exceeded.



&#x20;   This is fixed in the new message protocol by specifying

&#x20;   a list of signatures, each task will then pop a task off the list

&#x20;   when sending the next message:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       execute\_task(message)

&#x20;       chain = embed\['chain']

&#x20;       if chain:

&#x20;           sig = maybe\_signature(chain.pop())

&#x20;           sig.apply\_async(chain=chain)



\- ``correlation\_id`` replaces ``task\_id`` field.



\- ``root\_id`` and ``parent\_id`` fields helps keep track of work-flows.



\- ``shadow`` lets you specify a different name for logs, monitors

&#x20; can be used for concepts like tasks that calls a function

&#x20; specified as argument:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       from celery.utils.imports import qualname



&#x20;       class PickleTask(Task):



&#x20;           def unpack\_args(self, fun, args=()):

&#x20;               return fun, args



&#x20;           def apply\_async(self, args, kwargs, \*\*options):

&#x20;               fun, real\_args = self.unpack\_args(\*args)

&#x20;               return super().apply\_async(

&#x20;                   (fun, real\_args, kwargs), shadow=qualname(fun), \*\*options

&#x20;               )



&#x20;       @app.task(base=PickleTask)

&#x20;       def call(fun, args, kwargs):

&#x20;           return fun(\*args, \*\*kwargs)





.. \_message-protocol-task-v1:

.. \_task-message-protocol-v1:



Version 1

\---------



In version 1 of the protocol all fields are stored in the message body:

meaning workers and intermediate consumers must deserialize the payload

to read the fields.



Message body

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



\* ``task``

&#x20;   :`string`:



&#x20;   Name of the task. \*\*required\*\*



\* ``id``

&#x20;   :`string`:



&#x20;   Unique id of the task (UUID). \*\*required\*\*



\* ``args``

&#x20;   :`list`:



&#x20;   List of arguments. Will be an empty list if not provided.



\* ``kwargs``

&#x20;   :`dictionary`:



&#x20;   Dictionary of keyword arguments. Will be an empty dictionary if not

&#x20;   provided.



\* ``retries``

&#x20;   :`int`:



&#x20;   Current number of times this task has been retried.

&#x20;   Defaults to `0` if not specified.



\* ``eta``

&#x20;   :`string` (ISO 8601):



&#x20;   Estimated time of arrival. This is the date and time in ISO 8601

&#x20;   format. If not provided the message isn't scheduled, but will be

&#x20;   executed asap.



\* ``expires``

&#x20;   :`string` (ISO 8601):



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 2.0.2



&#x20;   Expiration date. This is the date and time in ISO 8601 format.

&#x20;   If not provided the message will never expire. The message

&#x20;   will be expired when the message is received and the expiration date

&#x20;   has been exceeded.



\* ``taskset``

&#x20;   :`string`:



&#x20;   The group this task is part of (if any).



\* ``chord``

&#x20;   :`Signature`:



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 2.3



&#x20;   Signifies that this task is one of the header parts of a chord. The value

&#x20;   of this key is the body of the cord that should be executed when all of

&#x20;   the tasks in the header has returned.



\* ``utc``

&#x20;   :`bool`:



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 2.5



&#x20;   If true time uses the UTC timezone, if not the current local timezone

&#x20;   should be used.



\* ``callbacks``

&#x20;   :`<list>Signature`:



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 3.0



&#x20;   A list of signatures to call if the task exited successfully.



\* ``errbacks``

&#x20;   :`<list>Signature`:



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 3.0



&#x20;   A list of signatures to call if an error occurs while executing the task.



\* ``timelimit``

&#x20;   :`<tuple>(float, float)`:



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 3.1



&#x20;   Task execution time limit settings. This is a tuple of hard and soft time

&#x20;   limit value (`int`/`float` or :const:`None` for no limit).



&#x20;   Example value specifying a soft time limit of 3 seconds, and a hard time

&#x20;   limit of 10 seconds::



&#x20;       {'timelimit': (3.0, 10.0)}





Example message

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



This is an example invocation of a `celery.task.ping` task in json

format:



.. code-block:: javascript



&#x20;   {"id": "4cc7438e-afd4-4f8f-a2f3-f46567e7ca77",

&#x20;    "task": "celery.task.PingTask",

&#x20;    "args": \[],

&#x20;    "kwargs": {},

&#x20;    "retries": 0,

&#x20;    "eta": "2009-11-17T12:30:56.527191"}



Task Serialization

\------------------



Several types of serialization formats are supported using the

`content\_type` message header.



The MIME-types supported by default are shown in the following table.



&#x20;   =============== =================================

&#x20;        Scheme                 MIME Type

&#x20;   =============== =================================

&#x20;   json            application/json

&#x20;   yaml            application/x-yaml

&#x20;   pickle          application/x-python-serialize

&#x20;   msgpack         application/x-msgpack

&#x20;   =============== =================================



.. \_message-protocol-event:



Event Messages

==============



Event messages are always JSON serialized and can contain arbitrary message

body fields.



Since version 4.0. the body can consist of either a single mapping (one event),

or a list of mappings (multiple events).



There are also standard fields that must always be present in an event

message:



Standard body fields

\--------------------



\- \*string\* ``type``



&#x20;   The type of event. This is a string containing the \*category\* and

&#x20;   \*action\* separated by a dash delimiter (e.g., ``task-succeeded``).



\- \*string\* ``hostname``



&#x20;   The fully qualified hostname of where the event occurred at.



\- \*unsigned long long\* ``clock``



&#x20;   The logical clock value for this event (Lamport time-stamp).



\- \*float\* ``timestamp``



&#x20;   The UNIX time-stamp corresponding to the time of when the event occurred.



\- \*signed short\* ``utcoffset``



&#x20;   This field describes the timezone of the originating host, and is

&#x20;   specified as the number of hours ahead of/behind UTC (e.g., -2 or

&#x20;   +1).



\- \*unsigned long long\* ``pid``



&#x20;   The process id of the process the event originated in.



Standard event types

\--------------------



For a list of standard event types and their fields see the

:ref:`event-reference`.



Example message

\---------------



This is the message fields for a ``task-succeeded`` event:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   properties = {

&#x20;       'routing\_key': 'task.succeeded',

&#x20;       'exchange': 'celeryev',

&#x20;       'content\_type': 'application/json',

&#x20;       'content\_encoding': 'utf-8',

&#x20;       'delivery\_mode': 1,

&#x20;   }

&#x20;   headers = {

&#x20;       'hostname': 'worker1@george.vandelay.com',

&#x20;   }

&#x20;   body = {

&#x20;       'type': 'task-succeeded',

&#x20;       'hostname': 'worker1@george.vandelay.com',

&#x20;       'pid': 6335,

&#x20;       'clock': 393912923921,

&#x20;       'timestamp': 1401717709.101747,

&#x20;       'utcoffset': -1,

&#x20;       'uuid': '9011d855-fdd1-4f8f-adb3-a413b499eafb',

&#x20;       'retval': '4',

&#x20;       'runtime': 0.0003212,

&#x20;   )



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\internals\\worker.rst

```rst

.. \_internals-worker:



=======================

&#x20;Internals: The worker

=======================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:



Introduction

============



The worker consists of 4 main components: the consumer, the scheduler,

the mediator and the task pool. All these components runs in parallel working

with two data structures: the ready queue and the ETA schedule.



Data structures

===============



timer

\-----



The timer uses :mod:`heapq` to schedule internal functions.

It's very efficient and can handle hundred of thousands of entries.





Components

==========



Consumer

\--------



Receives messages from the broker using :pypi:`Kombu`.



When a message is received it's converted into a

:class:`celery.worker.request.Request` object.



Tasks with an ETA, or rate-limit are entered into the `timer`,

messages that can be immediately processed are sent to the execution pool.



ETA and rate-limit when used together will result in the rate limit being

observed with the task being scheduled after the ETA.



Timer

\-----



The timer schedules internal functions, like cleanup and internal monitoring,

but also it schedules ETA tasks and rate limited tasks.

If the scheduled tasks ETA has passed it is moved to the execution pool.



TaskPool

\--------



This is a slightly modified :class:`multiprocessing.Pool`.

It mostly works the same way, except it makes sure all of the workers

are running at all times. If a worker is missing, it replaces

it with a new one.



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\reference\\celery.app.amqp.rst

```rst

.. currentmodule:: celery.app.amqp



.. automodule:: celery.app.amqp



&#x20;   .. contents::

&#x20;       :local:



&#x20;   AMQP

&#x20;   ----



&#x20;   .. autoclass:: AMQP



&#x20;       .. attribute:: Connection



&#x20;           Broker connection class used. Default is :class:`kombu.Connection`.



&#x20;       .. attribute:: Consumer



&#x20;           Base Consumer class used. Default is :class:`kombu.Consumer`.



&#x20;       .. attribute:: Producer



&#x20;           Base Producer class used. Default is :class:`kombu.Producer`.



&#x20;       .. attribute:: queues



&#x20;           All currently defined task queues (a :class:`Queues` instance).



&#x20;       .. attribute:: argsrepr\_maxsize



&#x20;           Max size of positional argument representation used for logging

&#x20;           purposes. Default is 1024.



&#x20;       .. attribute:: kwargsrepr\_maxsize



&#x20;           Max size of keyword argument representation used for logging

&#x20;           purposes. Default is 1024.



&#x20;       .. automethod:: Queues

&#x20;       .. automethod:: Router

&#x20;       .. automethod:: flush\_routes



&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: create\_task\_message

&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: send\_task\_message

&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: default\_queue

&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: default\_exchange

&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: producer\_pool

&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: router

&#x20;       .. autoattribute:: routes



&#x20;   Queues

&#x20;   ------



&#x20;   .. autoclass:: Queues

&#x20;       :members:

&#x20;       :undoc-members:



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\reference\\celery.rst

```rst

===========================================

&#x20;:mod:`celery` --- Distributed processing

===========================================



.. currentmodule:: celery

.. module:: celery

&#x20;   :synopsis: Distributed processing

.. moduleauthor:: Ask Solem <ask@celeryproject.org>

.. sectionauthor:: Ask Solem <ask@celeryproject.org>



\--------------



This module is the main entry-point for the Celery API.

It includes commonly needed things for calling tasks,

and creating Celery applications.



===================== ===================================================

:class:`Celery`       Celery application instance

:class:`group`        group tasks together

:class:`chain`        chain tasks together

:class:`chord`        chords enable callbacks for groups

:func:`signature`     create a new task signature

:class:`Signature`    object describing a task invocation

:data:`current\_app`   proxy to the current application instance

:data:`current\_task`  proxy to the currently executing task

===================== ===================================================



:class:`Celery` application objects

\-----------------------------------



.. versionadded:: 2.5



.. autoclass:: Celery





&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: user\_options



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: steps



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: current\_task



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: current\_worker\_task



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: amqp



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: backend



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: loader



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: control

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: events

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: log

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: tasks

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: pool

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: producer\_pool

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: Task

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: timezone

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: builtin\_fixups

&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: oid



&#x20;   .. automethod:: close



&#x20;   .. automethod:: signature



&#x20;   .. automethod:: bugreport



&#x20;   .. automethod:: config\_from\_object



&#x20;   .. automethod:: config\_from\_envvar



&#x20;   .. automethod:: autodiscover\_tasks



&#x20;   .. automethod:: add\_defaults



&#x20;   .. automethod:: add\_periodic\_task



&#x20;   .. automethod:: setup\_security



&#x20;   .. automethod:: task



&#x20;   .. automethod:: send\_task



&#x20;   .. automethod:: gen\_task\_name



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: AsyncResult



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: GroupResult



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: Worker



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: WorkController



&#x20;   .. autoattribute:: Beat



&#x20;   .. automethod:: connection\_for\_read



&#x20;   .. automethod:: connection\_for\_write



&#x20;   .. automethod:: connection



&#x20;   .. automethod:: connection\_or\_acquire



&#x20;   .. automethod:: producer\_or\_acquire



&#x20;   .. automethod:: select\_queues



&#x20;   .. automethod:: now



&#x20;   .. automethod:: set\_current



&#x20;   .. automethod:: set\_default



&#x20;   .. automethod:: finalize



&#x20;   .. automethod:: on\_init



&#x20;   .. automethod:: prepare\_config



&#x20;   .. data:: on\_configure



&#x20;       Signal sent when app is loading configuration.



&#x20;   .. data:: on\_after\_configure



&#x20;       Signal sent after app has prepared the configuration.



&#x20;   .. data:: on\_after\_finalize



&#x20;       Signal sent after app has been finalized.



&#x20;   .. data:: on\_after\_fork



&#x20;       Signal sent in child process after fork.



Canvas primitives

\-----------------



See :ref:`guide-canvas` for more about creating task work-flows.



.. autoclass:: group



.. autoclass:: chain



.. autoclass:: chord



.. autofunction:: signature



.. autoclass:: Signature



Proxies

\-------



.. data:: current\_app



&#x20;   The currently set app for this thread.



.. data:: current\_task



&#x20;   The task currently being executed

&#x20;   (only set in the worker, or when eager/apply is used).



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\reference\\celery.utils.debug.rst

```rst

====================================

&#x20;``celery.utils.debug``

====================================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:



Sampling Memory Usage

=====================



This module can be used to diagnose and sample the memory usage

used by parts of your application.



For example, to sample the memory usage of calling tasks you can do this:



.. code-block:: python





&#x20;   from celery.utils.debug import sample\_mem, memdump



&#x20;   from tasks import add





&#x20;   try:

&#x20;       for i in range(100):

&#x20;           for j in range(100):

&#x20;               add.delay(i, j)

&#x20;           sample\_mem()

&#x20;   finally:

&#x20;       memdump()





API Reference

=============



.. currentmodule:: celery.utils.debug



.. automodule:: celery.utils.debug



&#x20;   .. autofunction:: sample\_mem



&#x20;   .. autofunction:: memdump



&#x20;   .. autofunction:: sample



&#x20;   .. autofunction:: mem\_rss



&#x20;   .. autofunction:: ps



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\reference\\cli.rst

```rst

=======================

&#x20;Command Line Interface

=======================



.. NOTE:: The prefix `CELERY\_` must be added to the names of the environment

&#x20;  variables described below. E.g., `APP` becomes `CELERY\_APP`.



.. click:: celery.bin.celery:celery

&#x20;  :prog: celery

&#x20;  :nested: full



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\sec\\CELERYSA-0001.txt

```txt

=========================================

&#x20;CELERYSA-0001: Celery Security Advisory

=========================================

:contact: security@celeryproject.org

:author: Ask Solem

:CVE id: CVE-2011-4356

:date: 2011-11-25 04:35:00 p.m. GMT



Details

=======



:package: celery

:vulnerability: privilege escalation

:problem type: local

:risk: medium

:bug-no: Celery #544

:versions-affected: 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4



Description

===========



The --uid and --gid arguments to the celeryd-multi,

celeryd\_detach, celerybeat and celeryev programs shipped

with Celery versions 2.1 and later wasn't handled properly:

only the effective user was changed, with the real id remaining

unchanged.



In practice for affected users the vulnerability means that malicious code

loaded in the worker process would be allowed to escalate privileges.



We take this issue seriously since the Pickle serializer used by

default makes it possible to execute arbitrary code.



We recommend that users takes steps to secure their systems so that

malicious users cannot abuse the message broker to send messages,

or disable the pickle serializer used in Celery so that arbitrary code

execution isn't possible.



Patches are now available for all maintained versions (see below),

and users are urged to upgrade, even if not directly

affected.



Systems affected

================



Users of Celery versions 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4; except the recently

released 2.2.8, 2.3.4, and 2.4.4, daemonizing the Celery programs

as the root user, using either:

&#x20;   1) the --uid or --gid arguments, or

&#x20;   2) the provided generic init-scripts with the environment variables

&#x20;      CELERYD\_USER or CELERYD\_GROUP defined,

are affected.



Users using the Debian init-scripts, CentOS init-scripts, macOS launchctl

scripts, Supervisor, or users not starting the programs as the root user

are \*not\* affected.



Solution

========



Users of the 2.4 series should upgrade to 2.4.4:



&#x20;   \* ``pip install -U celery``, or

&#x20;   \* ``easy\_install -U celery``, or

&#x20;   \* https://pypi.org/project/celery/2.4.4/



Users of the 2.3 series should upgrade to 2.3.4:



&#x20;   \* ``pip install -U celery==2.3.4``, or

&#x20;   \* ``easy\_install -U celery==2.3.4``, or

&#x20;   \* https://pypi.org/project/celery/2.3.4/



Users of the 2.2 series should upgrade to 2.2.8:



&#x20;   \* ``pip install -U celery==2.2.8``, or

&#x20;   \* ``easy\_install -U celery==2.2.8``, or

&#x20;   \* https://pypi.org/project/celery/2.2.8/



The 2.1 series is no longer being maintained, so we urge users

of that series to upgrade to a more recent version.



Distribution package maintainers are urged to provide their users

with updated packages.





Please direct questions to the celery-users mailing-list:

https://groups.google.com/group/celery-users/,



or if you're planning to report a security issue we request that

you keep the information confidential by contacting

security@celeryproject.org, so that a fix can be issued as quickly as possible.



Thank you!



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\sec\\CELERYSA-0002.txt

```txt

=========================================

&#x20;CELERYSA-0002: Celery Security Advisory

=========================================

:contact: security@celeryproject.org

:CVE id: TBA

:date: 2014-07-10 05:00:00 p.m. UTC



Details

=======



:package: celery

:vulnerability: Environment error

:problem type: local

:risk: low

:versions-affected: 2.5, 3.0, 3.1



Description

===========



The built-in utility used to daemonize the Celery worker service sets

an insecure umask by default (umask 0).



This means that any files or directories created by the worker will

end up having world-writable permissions.



In practice this means that local users will be able to modify and possibly

corrupt the files created by user tasks.



This isn't immediately exploitable but can be if those files are later

evaluated as a program, for example a task that creates Python program files

that are later executed.



Patches are now available for all maintained versions (see below),

and users are urged to upgrade, even if not directly

affected.



Acknowledgments

===============



Special thanks to Red Hat for originally discovering and reporting the issue.



Systems affected

================



Users of Celery versions 3.0, and 3.1, except the recently

released 3.1.13, are affected if daemonizing the

Celery programs using the `--detach` argument or using the `celery multi` program

to start workers in the background, without setting a custom `--umask`

argument.



Solution

========



NOTE:

&#x20;   Not all users of Celery will use it to create files, but if you do

&#x20;   then files may already have been created with insecure permissions.



&#x20;   So after upgrading, or using the workaround, then please make sure

&#x20;   that files already created aren't world writable.



To work around the issue you can set a custom umask using the ``--umask``

argument:



&#x20;   $ celery worker -l info --detach --umask=18   # (022)



Or you can upgrade to a more recent version:



\- Users of the 3.1 series should upgrade to 3.1.13:



&#x20;   \* ``pip install -U celery``, or

&#x20;   \* ``easy\_install -U celery``, or

&#x20;   \* https://pypi.org/project/celery/3.1.13/



\- Users of the 3.0 series should upgrade to 3.0.25:



&#x20;   \* ``pip install -U celery==3.0.25``, or

&#x20;   \* ``easy\_install -U celery==3.0.25``, or

&#x20;   \* https://pypi.org/project/celery/3.0.25/



Distribution package maintainers are urged to provide their users

with updated packages.



Please direct questions to the celery-users mailing-list:

https://groups.google.com/group/celery-users/,



or if you're planning to report a new security related issue we request that

you keep the information confidential by contacting

security@celeryproject.org instead.



Thank you!



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\sec\\CELERYSA-0003.txt

```txt

=========================================

&#x20;CELERYSA-0003: Celery Security Advisory

=========================================

:contact: security@celeryproject.org

:CVE id: TBA

:date: 2016-12-08 05:00:00 p.m. PST



Details

=======



:package: celery

:vulnerability: Configuration Error

:problem type: remote

:risk: low

:versions-affected: 4.0.0



Description

===========



The default configuration in Celery 4.0.0 allowed for deserialization

of pickled messages, even if the software is configured to send

messages in the JSON format.



The particular configuration in question is the `accept\_content` setting,

which by default was set to:



&#x20;   app.conf.accept\_content = \['json', 'pickle', 'msgpack', 'yaml']



The risk is still set to low considering that an attacker would require access

to the message broker used to send messages to Celery workers.



Systems affected

================



Users of Celery version 4.0.0 with no explicit accept\_content setting set.



Solution

========



To work around the issue you can explicitly configure the accept\_content

setting:



&#x20;   app.conf.accept\_content = \['json']



Or you can upgrade to the Celery 4.0.1 version:



&#x20;   $ pip install -U celery



Distribution package maintainers are urged to provide their users

with updated packages.



Please direct questions to the celery-users mailing-list:

https://groups.google.com/group/celery-users/,



or if you're planning to report a new security related issue we request that

you keep the information confidential by contacting

security@celeryproject.org instead.



Thank you!



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\tutorials\\task-cookbook.rst

```rst

.. \_cookbook-tasks:



================

&#x20;Task Cookbook

================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:



.. \_cookbook-task-serial:



Ensuring a task is only executed one at a time

==============================================



You can accomplish this by using a lock.



In this example we'll be using the cache framework to set a lock that's

accessible for all workers.



It's part of an imaginary RSS feed importer called `djangofeeds`.

The task takes a feed URL as a single argument, and imports that feed into

a Django model called `Feed`. We ensure that it's not possible for two or

more workers to import the same feed at the same time by setting a cache key

consisting of the MD5 check-sum of the feed URL.



The cache key expires after some time in case something unexpected happens,

and something always will...



For this reason your tasks run-time shouldn't exceed the timeout.





.. note::



&#x20;   In order for this to work correctly you need to be using a cache

&#x20;   backend where the ``.add`` operation is atomic.  ``memcached`` is known

&#x20;   to work well for this purpose.



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   import time

&#x20;   from celery import task

&#x20;   from celery.utils.log import get\_task\_logger

&#x20;   from contextlib import contextmanager

&#x20;   from django.core.cache import cache

&#x20;   from hashlib import md5

&#x20;   from djangofeeds.models import Feed



&#x20;   logger = get\_task\_logger(\_\_name\_\_)



&#x20;   LOCK\_EXPIRE = 60 \* 10  # Lock expires in 10 minutes



&#x20;   @contextmanager

&#x20;   def memcache\_lock(lock\_id, oid):

&#x20;       timeout\_at = time.monotonic() + LOCK\_EXPIRE - 3

&#x20;       # cache.add fails if the key already exists

&#x20;       status = cache.add(lock\_id, oid, LOCK\_EXPIRE)

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           yield status

&#x20;       finally:

&#x20;           # memcache delete is very slow, but we have to use it to take

&#x20;           # advantage of using add() for atomic locking

&#x20;           if time.monotonic() < timeout\_at and status:

&#x20;               # don't release the lock if we exceeded the timeout

&#x20;               # to lessen the chance of releasing an expired lock

&#x20;               # owned by someone else

&#x20;               # also don't release the lock if we didn't acquire it

&#x20;               cache.delete(lock\_id)



&#x20;   @task(bind=True)

&#x20;   def import\_feed(self, feed\_url):

&#x20;       # The cache key consists of the task name and the MD5 digest

&#x20;       # of the feed URL.

&#x20;       feed\_url\_hexdigest = md5(feed\_url).hexdigest()

&#x20;       lock\_id = '{0}-lock-{1}'.format(self.name, feed\_url\_hexdigest)

&#x20;       logger.debug('Importing feed: %s', feed\_url)

&#x20;       with memcache\_lock(lock\_id, self.app.oid) as acquired:

&#x20;           if acquired:

&#x20;               return Feed.objects.import\_feed(feed\_url).url

&#x20;       logger.debug(

&#x20;           'Feed %s is already being imported by another worker', feed\_url)



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\application.rst

```rst

.. \_guide-app:



=============

&#x20;Application

=============



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:

&#x20;   :depth: 1



The Celery library must be instantiated before use, this instance

is called an application (or \*app\* for short).



The application is thread-safe so that multiple Celery applications

with different configurations, components, and tasks can co-exist in the

same process space.



Let's create one now:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from celery import Celery

&#x20;   >>> app = Celery()

&#x20;   >>> app

&#x20;   <Celery \_\_main\_\_:0x100469fd0>



The last line shows the textual representation of the application:

including the name of the app class (``Celery``), the name of the

current main module (``\_\_main\_\_``), and the memory address of the object

(``0x100469fd0``).



Main Name

=========



Only one of these is important, and that's the main module name.

Let's look at why that is.



When you send a task message in Celery, that message won't contain

any source code, but only the name of the task you want to execute.

This works similarly to how host names work on the internet: every worker

maintains a mapping of task names to their actual functions, called the \*task

registry\*.



Whenever you define a task, that task will also be added to the local registry:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> @app.task

&#x20;   ... def add(x, y):

&#x20;   ...     return x + y



&#x20;   >>> add

&#x20;   <@task: \_\_main\_\_.add>



&#x20;   >>> add.name

&#x20;   \_\_main\_\_.add



&#x20;   >>> app.tasks\['\_\_main\_\_.add']

&#x20;   <@task: \_\_main\_\_.add>



and there you see that ``\_\_main\_\_`` again; whenever Celery isn't able

to detect what module the function belongs to, it uses the main module

name to generate the beginning of the task name.



This is only a problem in a limited set of use cases:



&#x20;   #. If the module that the task is defined in is run as a program.

&#x20;   #. If the application is created in the Python shell (REPL).



For example here, where the tasks module is also used to start a worker

with :meth:`@worker\_main`:



:file:`tasks.py`:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery

&#x20;   app = Celery()



&#x20;   @app.task

&#x20;   def add(x, y): return x + y



&#x20;   if \_\_name\_\_ == '\_\_main\_\_':

&#x20;       args = \['worker', '--loglevel=INFO']

&#x20;       app.worker\_main(argv=args)



When this module is executed the tasks will be named starting with "``\_\_main\_\_``",

but when the module is imported by another process, say to call a task,

the tasks will be named starting with "``tasks``" (the real name of the module):



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from tasks import add

&#x20;   >>> add.name

&#x20;   tasks.add



You can specify another name for the main module:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> app = Celery('tasks')

&#x20;   >>> app.main

&#x20;   'tasks'



&#x20;   >>> @app.task

&#x20;   ... def add(x, y):

&#x20;   ...     return x + y



&#x20;   >>> add.name

&#x20;   tasks.add



.. seealso:: :ref:`task-names`



Configuration

=============



There are several options you can set that'll change how

Celery works. These options can be set directly on the app instance,

or you can use a dedicated configuration module.



The configuration is available as :attr:`@conf`:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> app.conf.timezone

&#x20;   'Europe/London'



where you can also set configuration values directly:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> app.conf.enable\_utc = True



or update several keys at once by using the ``update`` method:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   >>> app.conf.update(

&#x20;   ...     enable\_utc=True,

&#x20;   ...     timezone='Europe/London',

&#x20;   ...)



The configuration object consists of multiple dictionaries

that are consulted in order:



&#x20;   #. Changes made at run-time.

&#x20;   #. The configuration module (if any)

&#x20;   #. The default configuration (:mod:`celery.app.defaults`).



You can even add new default sources by using the :meth:`@add\_defaults`

method.



.. seealso::



&#x20;   Go to the :ref:`Configuration reference <configuration>` for a complete

&#x20;   listing of all the available settings, and their default values.



``config\_from\_object``

\----------------------



The :meth:`@config\_from\_object` method loads configuration

from a configuration object.



This can be a configuration module, or any object with configuration attributes.



Note that any configuration that was previously set will be reset when

:meth:`\~@config\_from\_object` is called. If you want to set additional

configuration you should do so after.



Example 1: Using the name of a module

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



The :meth:`@config\_from\_object` method can take the fully qualified

name of a Python module, or even the name of a Python attribute,

for example: ``"celeryconfig"``, ``"myproj.config.celery"``, or

``"myproj.config:CeleryConfig"``:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery



&#x20;   app = Celery()

&#x20;   app.config\_from\_object('celeryconfig')



The ``celeryconfig`` module may then look like this:



:file:`celeryconfig.py`:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   enable\_utc = True

&#x20;   timezone = 'Europe/London'



and the app will be able to use it as long as ``import celeryconfig`` is

possible.



Example 2: Passing an actual module object

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



You can also pass an already imported module object, but this

isn't always recommended.



.. tip::



&#x20;   Using the name of a module is recommended as this means the module does

&#x20;   not need to be serialized when the prefork pool is used. If you're

&#x20;   experiencing configuration problems or pickle errors then please

&#x20;   try using the name of a module instead.



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   import celeryconfig



&#x20;   from celery import Celery



&#x20;   app = Celery()

&#x20;   app.config\_from\_object(celeryconfig)





Example 3:  Using a configuration class/object

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery



&#x20;   app = Celery()



&#x20;   class Config:

&#x20;       enable\_utc = True

&#x20;       timezone = 'Europe/London'



&#x20;   app.config\_from\_object(Config)

&#x20;   # or using the fully qualified name of the object:

&#x20;   #   app.config\_from\_object('module:Config')



``config\_from\_envvar``

\----------------------



The :meth:`@config\_from\_envvar` takes the configuration module name

from an environment variable



For example -- to load configuration from a module specified in the

environment variable named :envvar:`CELERY\_CONFIG\_MODULE`:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   import os

&#x20;   from celery import Celery



&#x20;   #: Set default configuration module name

&#x20;   os.environ.setdefault('CELERY\_CONFIG\_MODULE', 'celeryconfig')



&#x20;   app = Celery()

&#x20;   app.config\_from\_envvar('CELERY\_CONFIG\_MODULE')



You can then specify the configuration module to use via the environment:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ CELERY\_CONFIG\_MODULE="celeryconfig.prod" celery worker -l INFO



.. \_app-censored-config:



Censored configuration

\----------------------



If you ever want to print out the configuration, as debugging information

or similar, you may also want to filter out sensitive information like

passwords and API keys.



Celery comes with several utilities useful for presenting the configuration,

one is :meth:`\~celery.app.utils.Settings.humanize`:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> app.conf.humanize(with\_defaults=False, censored=True)



This method returns the configuration as a tabulated string. This will

only contain changes to the configuration by default, but you can include the

built-in default keys and values by enabling the ``with\_defaults`` argument.



If you instead want to work with the configuration as a dictionary, you

can use the :meth:`\~celery.app.utils.Settings.table` method:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> app.conf.table(with\_defaults=False, censored=True)



Please note that Celery won't be able to remove all sensitive information,

as it merely uses a regular expression to search for commonly named keys.

If you add custom settings containing sensitive information you should name

the keys using a name that Celery identifies as secret.



A configuration setting will be censored if the name contains any of

these sub-strings:



``API``, ``TOKEN``, ``KEY``, ``SECRET``, ``PASS``, ``SIGNATURE``, ``DATABASE``



Laziness

========



The application instance is lazy, meaning it won't be evaluated

until it's actually needed.



Creating a :class:`@Celery` instance will only do the following:



&#x20;   #. Create a logical clock instance, used for events.

&#x20;   #. Create the task registry.

&#x20;   #. Set itself as the current app (but not if the ``set\_as\_current``

&#x20;      argument was disabled)

&#x20;   #. Call the :meth:`@on\_init` callback (does nothing by default).



The :meth:`@task` decorators don't create the tasks at the point when

the task is defined, instead it'll defer the creation

of the task to happen either when the task is used, or after the

application has been \*finalized\*,



This example shows how the task isn't created until

you use the task, or access an attribute (in this case :meth:`repr`):



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> @app.task

&#x20;   >>> def add(x, y):

&#x20;   ...    return x + y



&#x20;   >>> type(add)

&#x20;   <class 'celery.local.PromiseProxy'>



&#x20;   >>> add.\_\_evaluated\_\_()

&#x20;   False



&#x20;   >>> add        # <-- causes repr(add) to happen

&#x20;   <@task: \_\_main\_\_.add>



&#x20;   >>> add.\_\_evaluated\_\_()

&#x20;   True



\*Finalization\* of the app happens either explicitly by calling

:meth:`@finalize` -- or implicitly by accessing the :attr:`@tasks`

attribute.



Finalizing the object will:



&#x20;   #. Copy tasks that must be shared between apps



&#x20;       Tasks are shared by default, but if the

&#x20;       ``shared`` argument to the task decorator is disabled,

&#x20;       then the task will be private to the app it's bound to.



&#x20;   #. Evaluate all pending task decorators.



&#x20;   #. Make sure all tasks are bound to the current app.



&#x20;       Tasks are bound to an app so that they can read default

&#x20;       values from the configuration.



.. \_default-app:



.. topic:: The "default app"



&#x20;   Celery didn't always have applications, it used to be that

&#x20;   there was only a module-based API. A compatibility API was

&#x20;   available at the old location until the release of Celery 5.0,

&#x20;   but has been removed.



&#x20;   Celery always creates a special app - the "default app",

&#x20;   and this is used if no custom application has been instantiated.



&#x20;   The :mod:`celery.task` module is no longer available. Use the

&#x20;   methods on the app instance, not the module based API:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       from celery.task import Task   # << OLD Task base class.



&#x20;       from celery import Task        # << NEW base class.





Breaking the chain

==================



While it's possible to depend on the current app

being set, the best practice is to always pass the app instance

around to anything that needs it.



I call this the "app chain", since it creates a chain

of instances depending on the app being passed.



The following example is considered bad practice:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import current\_app



&#x20;   class Scheduler:



&#x20;       def run(self):

&#x20;           app = current\_app



Instead it should take the ``app`` as an argument:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   class Scheduler:



&#x20;       def \_\_init\_\_(self, app):

&#x20;           self.app = app



Internally Celery uses the :func:`celery.app.app\_or\_default` function

so that everything also works in the module-based compatibility API



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery.app import app\_or\_default



&#x20;   class Scheduler:

&#x20;       def \_\_init\_\_(self, app=None):

&#x20;           self.app = app\_or\_default(app)



In development you can set the :envvar:`CELERY\_TRACE\_APP`

environment variable to raise an exception if the app

chain breaks:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ CELERY\_TRACE\_APP=1 celery worker -l INFO





.. topic:: Evolving the API



&#x20;   Celery has changed a lot from 2009 since it was initially

&#x20;   created.



&#x20;   For example, in the beginning it was possible to use any callable as

&#x20;   a task:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       def hello(to):

&#x20;           return 'hello {0}'.format(to)



&#x20;       >>> from celery.execute import apply\_async



&#x20;       >>> apply\_async(hello, ('world!',))



&#x20;   or you could also create a ``Task`` class to set

&#x20;   certain options, or override other behavior



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       from celery import Task

&#x20;       from celery.registry import tasks



&#x20;       class Hello(Task):

&#x20;           queue = 'hipri'



&#x20;           def run(self, to):

&#x20;               return 'hello {0}'.format(to)

&#x20;       tasks.register(Hello)



&#x20;       >>> Hello.delay('world!')



&#x20;   Later, it was decided that passing arbitrary call-able's

&#x20;   was an anti-pattern, since it makes it very hard to use

&#x20;   serializers other than pickle, and the feature was removed

&#x20;   in 2.0, replaced by task decorators:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       from celery import app



&#x20;       @app.task(queue='hipri')

&#x20;       def hello(to):

&#x20;           return 'hello {0}'.format(to)



Abstract Tasks

==============



All tasks created using the :meth:`@task` decorator

will inherit from the application's base :attr:`\~@Task` class.



You can specify a different base class using the ``base`` argument:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   @app.task(base=OtherTask):

&#x20;   def add(x, y):

&#x20;       return x + y



To create a custom task class you should inherit from the neutral base

class: :class:`celery.Task`.



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Task



&#x20;   class DebugTask(Task):



&#x20;       def \_\_call\_\_(self, \*args, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;           print('TASK STARTING: {0.name}\[{0.request.id}]'.format(self))

&#x20;           return self.run(\*args, \*\*kwargs)





.. tip::



&#x20;   If you override the task's ``\_\_call\_\_`` method, then it's very important

&#x20;   that you also call ``self.run`` to execute the body of the task.  Do not

&#x20;   call ``super().\_\_call\_\_``.  The ``\_\_call\_\_`` method of the neutral base

&#x20;   class :class:`celery.Task` is only present for reference.  For optimization,

&#x20;   this has been unrolled into ``celery.app.trace.build\_tracer.trace\_task``

&#x20;   which calls ``run`` directly on the custom task class if no ``\_\_call\_\_``

&#x20;   method is defined.



The neutral base class is special because it's not bound to any specific app

yet. Once a task is bound to an app it'll read configuration to set default

values, and so on.



To realize a base class you need to create a task using the :meth:`@task`

decorator:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   @app.task(base=DebugTask)

&#x20;   def add(x, y):

&#x20;       return x + y



It's even possible to change the default base class for an application

by changing its :meth:`@Task` attribute:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from celery import Celery, Task



&#x20;   >>> app = Celery()



&#x20;   >>> class MyBaseTask(Task):

&#x20;   ...    queue = 'hipri'



&#x20;   >>> app.Task = MyBaseTask

&#x20;   >>> app.Task

&#x20;   <unbound MyBaseTask>



&#x20;   >>> @app.task

&#x20;   ... def add(x, y):

&#x20;   ...     return x + y



&#x20;   >>> add

&#x20;   <@task: \_\_main\_\_.add>



&#x20;   >>> add.\_\_class\_\_.mro()

&#x20;   \[<class add of <Celery \_\_main\_\_:0x1012b4410>>,

&#x20;    <unbound MyBaseTask>,

&#x20;    <unbound Task>,

&#x20;    <type 'object'>]



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\calling.rst

```rst

.. \_guide-calling:



===============

&#x20;Calling Tasks

===============



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:

&#x20;   :depth: 1





.. \_calling-basics:



Basics

======



This document describes Celery's uniform "Calling API"

used by task instances and the :ref:`canvas <guide-canvas>`.



The API defines a standard set of execution options, as well as three methods:



&#x20;   - ``apply\_async(args\[, kwargs\[, …]])``



&#x20;       Sends a task message.



&#x20;   - ``delay(\*args, \*\*kwargs)``



&#x20;       Shortcut to send a task message, but doesn't support execution

&#x20;       options.



&#x20;   - \*calling\* (``\_\_call\_\_``)



&#x20;       Applying an object supporting the calling API (e.g., ``add(2, 2)``)

&#x20;       means that the task will not be executed by a worker, but in the current

&#x20;       process instead (a message won't be sent).



.. \_calling-cheat:



.. topic:: Quick Cheat Sheet



&#x20;   - ``T.delay(arg, kwarg=value)``

&#x20;       Star arguments shortcut to ``.apply\_async``.

&#x20;       (``.delay(\*args, \*\*kwargs)`` calls ``.apply\_async(args, kwargs)``).



&#x20;   - ``T.apply\_async((arg,), {'kwarg': value})``



&#x20;   - ``T.apply\_async(countdown=10)``

&#x20;       executes in 10 seconds from now.



&#x20;   - ``T.apply\_async(eta=now + timedelta(seconds=10))``

&#x20;       executes in 10 seconds from now, specified using ``eta``



&#x20;   - ``T.apply\_async(countdown=60, expires=120)``

&#x20;       executes in one minute from now, but expires after 2 minutes.



&#x20;   - ``T.apply\_async(expires=now + timedelta(days=2))``

&#x20;       expires in 2 days, set using :class:`\~datetime.datetime`.





Example

\-------



The :meth:`\~@Task.delay` method is convenient as it looks like calling a regular

function:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   task.delay(arg1, arg2, kwarg1='x', kwarg2='y')



Using :meth:`\~@Task.apply\_async` instead you have to write:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   task.apply\_async(args=\[arg1, arg2], kwargs={'kwarg1': 'x', 'kwarg2': 'y'})



.. sidebar:: Tip



&#x20;   If the task isn't registered in the current process

&#x20;   you can use :meth:`\~@send\_task` to call the task by name instead.





So `delay` is clearly convenient, but if you want to set additional execution

options you have to use ``apply\_async``.



The rest of this document will go into the task execution

options in detail. All examples use a task

called `add`, returning the sum of two arguments:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   @app.task

&#x20;   def add(x, y):

&#x20;       return x + y





.. topic:: There's another way…



&#x20;   You'll learn more about this later while reading about the :ref:`Canvas

&#x20;   <guide-canvas>`, but :class:`\~celery.signature`'s are objects used to pass around

&#x20;   the signature of a task invocation, (for example to send it over the

&#x20;   network), and they also support the Calling API:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       task.s(arg1, arg2, kwarg1='x', kwargs2='y').apply\_async()



.. \_calling-links:



Linking (callbacks/errbacks)

============================



Celery supports linking tasks together so that one task follows another.

The callback task will be applied with the result of the parent task

as a partial argument:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   add.apply\_async((2, 2), link=add.s(16))



.. sidebar:: What's ``s``?



&#x20;   The ``add.s`` call used here is called a signature. If you

&#x20;   don't know what they are you should read about them in the

&#x20;   :ref:`canvas guide <guide-canvas>`.

&#x20;   There you can also learn about :class:`\~celery.chain`:  a simpler

&#x20;   way to chain tasks together.



&#x20;   In practice the ``link`` execution option is considered an internal

&#x20;   primitive, and you'll probably not use it directly, but

&#x20;   use chains instead.



Here the result of the first task (4) will be sent to a new

task that adds 16 to the previous result, forming the expression

:math:`(2 + 2) + 16 = 20`





You can also cause a callback to be applied if task raises an exception

(\*errback\*). The worker won't actually call the errback as a task, but will

instead call the errback function directly so that the raw request, exception

and traceback objects can be passed to it.



This is an example error callback:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   @app.task

&#x20;   def error\_handler(request, exc, traceback):

&#x20;       print('Task {0} raised exception: {1!r}\\n{2!r}'.format(

&#x20;             request.id, exc, traceback))



it can be added to the task using the ``link\_error`` execution

option:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   add.apply\_async((2, 2), link\_error=error\_handler.s())





In addition, both the ``link`` and ``link\_error`` options can be expressed

as a list:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   add.apply\_async((2, 2), link=\[add.s(16), other\_task.s()])



The callbacks/errbacks will then be called in order, and all

callbacks will be called with the return value of the parent task

as a partial argument.



In the case of a chord, we can handle errors using multiple handling strategies.

See :ref:`chord error handling <chord-errors>` for more information.



.. \_calling-on-message:



On message

==========



Celery supports catching all states changes by setting on\_message callback.



For example for long-running tasks to send task progress you can do something like this:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   @app.task(bind=True)

&#x20;   def hello(self, a, b):

&#x20;       time.sleep(1)

&#x20;       self.update\_state(state="PROGRESS", meta={'progress': 50})

&#x20;       time.sleep(1)

&#x20;       self.update\_state(state="PROGRESS", meta={'progress': 90})

&#x20;       time.sleep(1)

&#x20;       return 'hello world: %i' % (a+b)



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   def on\_raw\_message(body):

&#x20;       print(body)



&#x20;   a, b = 1, 1

&#x20;   r = hello.apply\_async(args=(a, b))

&#x20;   print(r.get(on\_message=on\_raw\_message, propagate=False))



Will generate output like this:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   {'task\_id': '5660d3a3-92b8-40df-8ccc-33a5d1d680d7',

&#x20;    'result': {'progress': 50},

&#x20;    'children': \[],

&#x20;    'status': 'PROGRESS',

&#x20;    'traceback': None}

&#x20;   {'task\_id': '5660d3a3-92b8-40df-8ccc-33a5d1d680d7',

&#x20;    'result': {'progress': 90},

&#x20;    'children': \[],

&#x20;    'status': 'PROGRESS',

&#x20;    'traceback': None}

&#x20;   {'task\_id': '5660d3a3-92b8-40df-8ccc-33a5d1d680d7',

&#x20;    'result': 'hello world: 10',

&#x20;    'children': \[],

&#x20;    'status': 'SUCCESS',

&#x20;    'traceback': None}

&#x20;   hello world: 10





.. \_calling-eta:



ETA and Countdown

=================



The ETA (estimated time of arrival) lets you set a specific date and time that

is the earliest time at which your task will be executed. `countdown` is

a shortcut to set ETA by seconds into the future.



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> result = add.apply\_async((2, 2), countdown=3)

&#x20;   >>> result.get()    # this takes at least 3 seconds to return

&#x20;   4



The task is guaranteed to be executed at some time \*after\* the

specified date and time, but not necessarily at that exact time.

Possible reasons for broken deadlines may include many items waiting

in the queue, or heavy network latency. To make sure your tasks

are executed in a timely manner you should monitor the queue for congestion. Use

Munin, or similar tools, to receive alerts, so appropriate action can be

taken to ease the workload. See :ref:`monitoring-munin`.



While `countdown` is an integer, `eta` must be a :class:`\~datetime.datetime`

object, specifying an exact date and time (including millisecond precision,

and timezone information):



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone



&#x20;   >>> tomorrow = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=1)

&#x20;   >>> add.apply\_async((2, 2), eta=tomorrow)



.. warning::



&#x20;   Tasks with `eta` or `countdown` are immediately fetched by the worker

&#x20;   and until the scheduled time passes, they reside in the worker's memory.

&#x20;   When using those options to schedule lots of tasks for a distant future,

&#x20;   those tasks may accumulate in the worker and make a significant impact on

&#x20;   the RAM usage.



&#x20;   Moreover, tasks are not acknowledged until the worker starts executing

&#x20;   them. If using Redis as a broker, task will get redelivered when `countdown`

&#x20;   exceeds `visibility\_timeout` (see :ref:`redis-caveats`).



&#x20;   Therefore, using `eta` and `countdown` \*\*is not recommended\*\* for

&#x20;   scheduling tasks for a distant future. Ideally, use values no longer

&#x20;   than several minutes. For longer durations, consider using

&#x20;   database-backed periodic tasks, e.g. with :pypi:`django-celery-beat` if

&#x20;   using Django (see :ref:`beat-custom-schedulers`).



.. warning::



&#x20;   When using RabbitMQ as a message broker when specifying a ``countdown``

&#x20;   over 15 minutes, you may encounter the problem that the worker terminates

&#x20;   with an :exc:`\~amqp.exceptions.PreconditionFailed` error will be raised:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       amqp.exceptions.PreconditionFailed: (0, 0): (406) PRECONDITION\_FAILED - consumer ack timed out on channel



&#x20;   In RabbitMQ since version 3.8.15 the default value for

&#x20;   ``consumer\_timeout`` is 15 minutes.

&#x20;   Since version 3.8.17 it was increased to 30 minutes. If a consumer does

&#x20;   not ack its delivery for more than the timeout value, its channel will be

&#x20;   closed with a ``PRECONDITION\_FAILED`` channel exception.

&#x20;   See `Delivery Acknowledgement Timeout`\_ for more information.



&#x20;   To solve the problem, in RabbitMQ configuration file ``rabbitmq.conf`` you

&#x20;   should specify the ``consumer\_timeout`` parameter greater than or equal to

&#x20;   your countdown value. For example, you can specify a very large value

&#x20;   of ``consumer\_timeout = 31622400000``, which is equal to 1 year

&#x20;   in milliseconds, to avoid problems in the future.



.. \_`Delivery Acknowledgement Timeout`: https://www.rabbitmq.com/consumers.html#acknowledgement-timeout



.. \_calling-expiration:



Expiration

==========



The `expires` argument defines an optional expiry time,

either as seconds after task publish, or a specific date and time using

:class:`\~datetime.datetime`:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> # Task expires after one minute from now.

&#x20;   >>> add.apply\_async((10, 10), expires=60)



&#x20;   >>> # Also supports datetime

&#x20;   >>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

&#x20;   >>> add.apply\_async((10, 10), kwargs,

&#x20;   ...                 expires=datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=1))





When a worker receives an expired task it will mark

the task as :state:`REVOKED` (:exc:`\~@TaskRevokedError`).



.. \_calling-retry:



Message Sending Retry

=====================



Celery will automatically retry sending messages in the event of connection

failure, and retry behavior can be configured -- like how often to retry, or a maximum

number of retries -- or disabled all together.



To disable retry you can set the ``retry`` execution option to :const:`False`:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   add.apply\_async((2, 2), retry=False)



.. topic:: Related Settings



&#x20;   .. hlist::

&#x20;       :columns: 2



&#x20;       - :setting:`task\_publish\_retry`

&#x20;       - :setting:`task\_publish\_retry\_policy`



Retry Policy

\------------



A retry policy is a mapping that controls how retries behave,

and can contain the following keys:



\- `max\_retries`



&#x20;   Maximum number of retries before giving up, in this case the

&#x20;   exception that caused the retry to fail will be raised.



&#x20;   A value of :const:`None` means it will retry forever.



&#x20;   The default is to retry 3 times.



\- `interval\_start`



&#x20;   Defines the number of seconds (float or integer) to wait between

&#x20;   retries. Default is 0 (the first retry will be instantaneous).



\- `interval\_step`



&#x20;   On each consecutive retry this number will be added to the retry

&#x20;   delay (float or integer). Default is 0.2.



\- `interval\_max`



&#x20;   Maximum number of seconds (float or integer) to wait between

&#x20;   retries. Default is 0.2.



\- `retry\_errors`



&#x20;   `retry\_errors` is a tuple of exception classes that should be retried.

&#x20;   It will be ignored if not specified. Default is None (ignored).



&#x20;   For example, if you want to retry only tasks that were timed out, you can use

&#x20;   :exc:`\~kombu.exceptions.TimeoutError`:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       from kombu.exceptions import TimeoutError



&#x20;       add.apply\_async((2, 2), retry=True, retry\_policy={

&#x20;           'max\_retries': 3,

&#x20;           'retry\_errors': (TimeoutError, ),

&#x20;       })



&#x20;   .. versionadded:: 5.3



For example, the default policy correlates to:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   add.apply\_async((2, 2), retry=True, retry\_policy={

&#x20;       'max\_retries': 3,

&#x20;       'interval\_start': 0,

&#x20;       'interval\_step': 0.2,

&#x20;       'interval\_max': 0.2,

&#x20;       'retry\_errors': None,

&#x20;   })



the maximum time spent retrying will be 0.4 seconds. It's set relatively

short by default because a connection failure could lead to a retry pile effect

if the broker connection is down -- For example, many web server processes waiting

to retry, blocking other incoming requests.



.. \_calling-connection-errors:



Connection Error Handling

=========================



When you send a task and the message transport connection is lost, or

the connection cannot be initiated, an :exc:`\~kombu.exceptions.OperationalError`

error will be raised:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from proj.tasks import add

&#x20;   >>> add.delay(2, 2)

&#x20;   Traceback (most recent call last):

&#x20;     File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

&#x20;     File "celery/app/task.py", line 388, in delay

&#x20;           return self.apply\_async(args, kwargs)

&#x20;     File "celery/app/task.py", line 503, in apply\_async

&#x20;       \*\*options

&#x20;     File "celery/app/base.py", line 662, in send\_task

&#x20;       amqp.send\_task\_message(P, name, message, \*\*options)

&#x20;     File "celery/backends/rpc.py", line 275, in on\_task\_call

&#x20;       maybe\_declare(self.binding(producer.channel), retry=True)

&#x20;     File "/opt/celery/kombu/kombu/messaging.py", line 204, in \_get\_channel

&#x20;       channel = self.\_channel = channel()

&#x20;     File "/opt/celery/py-amqp/amqp/connection.py", line 272, in connect

&#x20;       self.transport.connect()

&#x20;     File "/opt/celery/py-amqp/amqp/transport.py", line 100, in connect

&#x20;       self.\_connect(self.host, self.port, self.connect\_timeout)

&#x20;     File "/opt/celery/py-amqp/amqp/transport.py", line 141, in \_connect

&#x20;       self.sock.connect(sa)

&#x20;     kombu.exceptions.OperationalError: \[Errno 61] Connection refused



If you have :ref:`retries <calling-retry>` enabled this will only happen after

retries are exhausted, or when disabled immediately.



You can handle this error too:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from celery.utils.log import get\_logger

&#x20;   >>> logger = get\_logger(\_\_name\_\_)



&#x20;   >>> try:

&#x20;   ...     add.delay(2, 2)

&#x20;   ... except add.OperationalError as exc:

&#x20;   ...     logger.exception('Sending task raised: %r', exc)



.. \_calling-serializers:



Serializers

===========



.. sidebar::  Security



&#x20;   The pickle module allows for execution of arbitrary functions,

&#x20;   please see the :ref:`security guide <guide-security>`.



&#x20;   Celery also comes with a special serializer that uses

&#x20;   cryptography to sign your messages.



Data transferred between clients and workers needs to be serialized,

so every message in Celery has a ``content\_type`` header that

describes the serialization method used to encode it.



The default serializer is `JSON`, but you can

change this using the :setting:`task\_serializer` setting,

or for each individual task, or even per message.



There's built-in support for `JSON`, :mod:`pickle`, `YAML`

and ``msgpack``, and you can also add your own custom serializers by registering

them into the Kombu serializer registry



.. seealso::



&#x20;   :ref:`Message Serialization <kombu:guide-serialization>` in the Kombu user

&#x20;   guide.



Each option has its advantages and disadvantages.



json -- JSON is supported in many programming languages, is now

&#x20;   a standard part of Python (since 2.6), and is fairly fast to decode.



&#x20;   The primary disadvantage to JSON is that it limits you to the following

&#x20;   data types: strings, Unicode, floats, Boolean, dictionaries, and lists.

&#x20;   Decimals and dates are notably missing.



&#x20;   Binary data will be transferred using Base64 encoding,

&#x20;   increasing the size of the transferred data by 34% compared to an encoding

&#x20;   format where native binary types are supported.



&#x20;   However, if your data fits inside the above constraints and you need

&#x20;   cross-language support, the default setting of JSON is probably your

&#x20;   best choice.



&#x20;   See http://json.org for more information.



&#x20;   .. note::



&#x20;       (From Python official docs https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/json.html)

&#x20;       Keys in key/value pairs of JSON are always of the type :class:`str`. When

&#x20;       a dictionary is converted into JSON, all the keys of the dictionary are

&#x20;       coerced to strings. As a result of this, if a dictionary is converted

&#x20;       into JSON and then back into a dictionary, the dictionary may not equal

&#x20;       the original one. That is, ``loads(dumps(x)) != x`` if x has non-string

&#x20;       keys.



&#x20;   .. warning::



&#x20;       With more complex workflows created using :ref:`guide-canvas`, the JSON

&#x20;       serializer has been observed to drastically inflate message sizes due to

&#x20;       recursive references, leading to resource issues. The \*pickle\* serializer

&#x20;       is not vulnerable to this and may therefore be preferable in such cases.



pickle -- If you have no desire to support any language other than

&#x20;   Python, then using the pickle encoding will gain you the support of

&#x20;   all built-in Python data types (except class instances), smaller

&#x20;   messages when sending binary files, and a slight speedup over JSON

&#x20;   processing.



&#x20;   See :mod:`pickle` for more information.



yaml -- YAML has many of the same characteristics as json,

&#x20;   except that it natively supports more data types (including dates,

&#x20;   recursive references, etc.).



&#x20;   However, the Python libraries for YAML are a good bit slower than the

&#x20;   libraries for JSON.



&#x20;   If you need a more expressive set of data types and need to maintain

&#x20;   cross-language compatibility, then YAML may be a better fit than the above.



&#x20;   To use it, install Celery with:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;     $ pip install celery\[yaml]



&#x20;   See http://yaml.org/ for more information.



msgpack -- msgpack is a binary serialization format that's closer to JSON

&#x20;   in features. The format compresses better, so is a faster to parse and

&#x20;   encode compared to JSON.



&#x20;   To use it, install Celery with:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;     $ pip install celery\[msgpack]



&#x20;   See http://msgpack.org/ for more information.



To use a custom serializer you need to add the content type to

:setting:`accept\_content`. By default, only JSON is accepted,

and tasks containing other content headers are rejected.



The following order is used to decide the serializer

used when sending a task:



&#x20;   1. The `serializer` execution option.

&#x20;   2. The :attr:`@-Task.serializer` attribute

&#x20;   3. The :setting:`task\_serializer` setting.





Example setting a custom serializer for a single task invocation:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> add.apply\_async((10, 10), serializer='json')



.. \_calling-compression:



Compression

===========



Celery can compress messages using the following builtin schemes:



\- `brotli`



&#x20;   brotli is optimized for the web, in particular small text

&#x20;   documents. It is most effective for serving static content

&#x20;   such as fonts and html pages.



&#x20;   To use it, install Celery with:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;     $ pip install celery\[brotli]



\- `bzip2`



&#x20;   bzip2 creates smaller files than gzip, but compression and

&#x20;   decompression speeds are noticeably slower than those of gzip.



&#x20;   To use it, please ensure your Python executable was compiled

&#x20;   with bzip2 support.



&#x20;   If you get the following :class:`ImportError`:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;     >>> import bz2

&#x20;     Traceback (most recent call last):

&#x20;       File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

&#x20;     ImportError: No module named 'bz2'



&#x20;   it means that you should recompile your Python version with bzip2 support.



\- `gzip`



&#x20;   gzip is suitable for systems that require a small memory footprint,

&#x20;   making it ideal for systems with limited memory. It is often

&#x20;   used to generate files with the ".tar.gz" extension.



&#x20;   To use it, please ensure your Python executable was compiled

&#x20;   with gzip support.



&#x20;   If you get the following :class:`ImportError`:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;     >>> import gzip

&#x20;     Traceback (most recent call last):

&#x20;       File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

&#x20;     ImportError: No module named 'gzip'



&#x20;   it means that you should recompile your Python version with gzip support.



\- `lzma`



&#x20;   lzma provides a good compression ratio and executes with

&#x20;   fast compression and decompression speeds at the expense

&#x20;   of higher memory usage.



&#x20;   To use it, please ensure your Python executable was compiled

&#x20;   with lzma support and that your Python version is 3.3 and above.



&#x20;   If you get the following :class:`ImportError`:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;     >>> import lzma

&#x20;     Traceback (most recent call last):

&#x20;       File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

&#x20;     ImportError: No module named 'lzma'



&#x20;   it means that you should recompile your Python version with lzma support.



&#x20;   Alternatively, you can also install a backport using:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;     $ pip install celery\[lzma]



\- `zlib`



&#x20;   zlib is an abstraction of the Deflate algorithm in library

&#x20;   form which includes support both for the gzip file format

&#x20;   and a lightweight stream format in its API. It is a crucial

&#x20;   component of many software systems - Linux kernel and Git VCS just

&#x20;   to name a few.



&#x20;   To use it, please ensure your Python executable was compiled

&#x20;   with zlib support.



&#x20;   If you get the following :class:`ImportError`:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;     >>> import zlib

&#x20;     Traceback (most recent call last):

&#x20;       File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

&#x20;     ImportError: No module named 'zlib'



&#x20;   it means that you should recompile your Python version with zlib support.



\- `zstd`



&#x20;   zstd targets real-time compression scenarios at zlib-level

&#x20;   and better compression ratios. It's backed by a very fast entropy

&#x20;   stage, provided by Huff0 and FSE library.



&#x20;   To use it, install Celery with:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;     $ pip install celery\[zstd]



You can also create your own compression schemes and register

them in the :func:`kombu compression registry <kombu.compression.register>`.



The following order is used to decide the compression scheme

used when sending a task:



&#x20;   1. The `compression` execution option.

&#x20;   2. The :attr:`@-Task.compression` attribute.

&#x20;   3. The :setting:`task\_compression` attribute.



Example specifying the compression used when calling a task::



&#x20;   >>> add.apply\_async((2, 2), compression='zlib')



.. \_calling-connections:



Connections

===========



.. sidebar:: Automatic Pool Support



&#x20;   Since version 2.3 there's support for automatic connection pools,

&#x20;   so you don't have to manually handle connections and publishers

&#x20;   to reuse connections.



&#x20;   The connection pool is enabled by default since version 2.5.



&#x20;   See the :setting:`broker\_pool\_limit` setting for more information.



You can handle the connection manually by creating a

publisher:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   numbers = \[(2, 2), (4, 4), (8, 8), (16, 16)]

&#x20;   results = \[]

&#x20;   with add.app.pool.acquire(block=True) as connection:

&#x20;       with add.get\_publisher(connection) as publisher:

&#x20;           try:

&#x20;               for i, j in numbers:

&#x20;                   res = add.apply\_async((i, j), publisher=publisher)

&#x20;                   results.append(res)

&#x20;   print(\[res.get() for res in results])





Though this particular example is much better expressed as a group:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> from celery import group



&#x20;   >>> numbers = \[(2, 2), (4, 4), (8, 8), (16, 16)]

&#x20;   >>> res = group(add.s(i, j) for i, j in numbers).apply\_async()



&#x20;   >>> res.get()

&#x20;   \[4, 8, 16, 32]



.. \_calling-routing:



Routing options

===============



Celery can route tasks to different queues.



Simple routing (name <-> name) is accomplished using the ``queue`` option::



&#x20;   add.apply\_async(queue='priority.high')



You can then assign workers to the ``priority.high`` queue by using

the workers :option:`-Q <celery worker -Q>` argument:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj worker -l INFO -Q celery,priority.high



.. seealso::



&#x20;   Hard-coding queue names in code isn't recommended, the best practice

&#x20;   is to use configuration routers (:setting:`task\_routes`).



&#x20;   To find out more about routing, please see :ref:`guide-routing`.



.. \_calling-results:



Results options

===============



You can enable or disable result storage using the :setting:`task\_ignore\_result`

setting or by using the ``ignore\_result`` option:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20; >>> result = add.apply\_async((1, 2), ignore\_result=True)

&#x20; >>> result.get()

&#x20; None



&#x20; >>> # Do not ignore result (default)

&#x20; ...

&#x20; >>> result = add.apply\_async((1, 2), ignore\_result=False)

&#x20; >>> result.get()

&#x20; 3



If you'd like to store additional metadata about the task in the result backend

set the :setting:`result\_extended` setting to ``True``.



.. seealso::



&#x20;  For more information on tasks, please see :ref:`guide-tasks`.



Advanced Options

\----------------



These options are for advanced users who want to take use of

AMQP's full routing capabilities. Interested parties may read the

:ref:`routing guide <guide-routing>`.



\- exchange



&#x20;   Name of exchange (or a :class:`kombu.entity.Exchange`) to

&#x20;   send the message to.



\- routing\_key



&#x20;   Routing key used to determine.



\- priority



&#x20;   A number between `0` and `255`, where `255` is the highest priority.



&#x20;   Supported by: RabbitMQ, Redis (priority reversed, 0 is highest).



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\canvas.rst

```rst

.. \_guide-canvas:



==============================

&#x20;Canvas: Designing Work-flows

==============================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:

&#x20;   :depth: 2



.. \_canvas-subtasks:



.. \_canvas-signatures:



Signatures

==========



.. versionadded:: 2.0



You just learned how to call a task using the tasks ``delay`` method

in the :ref:`calling <guide-calling>` guide, and this is often all you need,

but sometimes you may want to pass the signature of a task invocation to

another process or as an argument to another function.



A :func:`\~celery.signature` wraps the arguments, keyword arguments, and execution options

of a single task invocation in a way such that it can be passed to functions

or even serialized and sent across the wire.



\- You can create a signature for the ``add`` task using its name like this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> from celery import signature

&#x20;       >>> signature('tasks.add', args=(2, 2), countdown=10)

&#x20;       tasks.add(2, 2)



&#x20; This task has a signature of arity 2 (two arguments): ``(2, 2)``,

&#x20; and sets the countdown execution option to 10.



\- or you can create one using the task's ``signature`` method:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> add.signature((2, 2), countdown=10)

&#x20;       tasks.add(2, 2)



\- There's also a shortcut using star arguments:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> add.s(2, 2)

&#x20;       tasks.add(2, 2)



\- Keyword arguments are also supported:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> add.s(2, 2, debug=True)

&#x20;       tasks.add(2, 2, debug=True)



\- From any signature instance you can inspect the different fields:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> s = add.signature((2, 2), {'debug': True}, countdown=10)

&#x20;       >>> s.args

&#x20;       (2, 2)

&#x20;       >>> s.kwargs

&#x20;       {'debug': True}

&#x20;       >>> s.options

&#x20;       {'countdown': 10}



\- It supports the "Calling API" of ``delay``,

&#x20; ``apply\_async``, etc., including being called directly (``\_\_call\_\_``).



&#x20;   Calling the signature will execute the task inline in the current process:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> add(2, 2)

&#x20;       4

&#x20;       >>> add.s(2, 2)()

&#x20;       4



&#x20;   ``delay`` is our beloved shortcut to ``apply\_async`` taking star-arguments:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> result = add.delay(2, 2)

&#x20;       >>> result.get()

&#x20;       4



&#x20;   ``apply\_async`` takes the same arguments as the

&#x20;   :meth:`Task.apply\_async <@Task.apply\_async>` method:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> add.apply\_async(args, kwargs, \*\*options)

&#x20;       >>> add.signature(args, kwargs, \*\*options).apply\_async()



&#x20;       >>> add.apply\_async((2, 2), countdown=1)

&#x20;       >>> add.signature((2, 2), countdown=1).apply\_async()



\- You can't define options with :meth:`\~@Task.s`, but a chaining

&#x20; ``set`` call takes care of that:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> add.s(2, 2).set(countdown=1)

&#x20;       proj.tasks.add(2, 2)



Partials

\--------



With a signature, you can execute the task in a worker:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> add.s(2, 2).delay()

&#x20;   >>> add.s(2, 2).apply\_async(countdown=1)



Or you can call it directly in the current process:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> add.s(2, 2)()

&#x20;   4



Specifying additional args, kwargs, or options to ``apply\_async``/``delay``

creates partials:



\- Any arguments added will be prepended to the args in the signature:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;       >>> partial = add.s(2)          # incomplete signature

&#x20;       >>> partial.delay(4)            # 4 + 2

&#x20;       >>> partial.apply\_async((4,))  # same
```
