```rst
See the Python :mod:`logging` module for more information about log

formats.



.. setting:: worker\_redirect\_stdouts



``worker\_redirect\_stdouts``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: Enabled by default.



If enabled `stdout` and `stderr` will be redirected

to the current logger.



Used by :program:`celery worker` and :program:`celery beat`.



.. setting:: worker\_redirect\_stdouts\_level



``worker\_redirect\_stdouts\_level``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: :const:`WARNING`.



The log level output to `stdout` and `stderr` is logged as.

Can be one of :const:`DEBUG`, :const:`INFO`, :const:`WARNING`,

:const:`ERROR`, or :const:`CRITICAL`.



.. \_conf-security:



Security

\--------



.. setting:: security\_key



``security\_key``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: :const:`None`.



.. versionadded:: 2.5



The relative or absolute path to a file containing the private key

used to sign messages when :ref:`message-signing` is used.



.. setting:: security\_key\_password



``security\_key\_password``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: :const:`None`.



.. versionadded:: 5.3.0



The password used to decrypt the private key when :ref:`message-signing`

is used.



.. setting:: security\_certificate



``security\_certificate``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: :const:`None`.



.. versionadded:: 2.5



The relative or absolute path to an X.509 certificate file

used to sign messages when :ref:`message-signing` is used.



.. setting:: security\_cert\_store



``security\_cert\_store``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: :const:`None`.



.. versionadded:: 2.5



The directory containing X.509 certificates used for

:ref:`message-signing`. Can be a glob with wild-cards,

(for example :file:`/etc/certs/\*.pem`).



.. setting:: security\_digest



``security\_digest``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: :const:`sha256`.



.. versionadded:: 4.3



A cryptography digest used to sign messages

when :ref:`message-signing` is used.

https://cryptography.io/en/latest/hazmat/primitives/cryptographic-hashes/#module-cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes



.. \_conf-custom-components:



Custom Component Classes (advanced)

\-----------------------------------



.. setting:: worker\_pool



``worker\_pool``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: ``"prefork"`` (``celery.concurrency.prefork:TaskPool``).



Name of the pool class used by the worker.



.. admonition:: Eventlet/Gevent



&#x20;   Never use this option to select the eventlet or gevent pool.

&#x20;   You must use the :option:`-P <celery worker -P>` option to

&#x20;   :program:`celery worker` instead, to ensure the monkey patches

&#x20;   aren't applied too late, causing things to break in strange ways.



.. setting:: worker\_pool\_restarts



``worker\_pool\_restarts``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: Disabled by default.



If enabled the worker pool can be restarted using the

:control:`pool\_restart` remote control command.



.. setting:: worker\_autoscaler



``worker\_autoscaler``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 2.2



Default: ``"celery.worker.autoscale:Autoscaler"``.



Name of the autoscaler class to use.



.. setting:: worker\_consumer



``worker\_consumer``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: ``"celery.worker.consumer:Consumer"``.



Name of the consumer class used by the worker.



.. setting:: worker\_timer



``worker\_timer``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: ``"kombu.asynchronous.hub.timer:Timer"``.



Name of the ETA scheduler class used by the worker.

Default is or set by the pool implementation.



.. setting:: worker\_logfile



``worker\_logfile``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional file path for :program:`celery worker` to log into (defaults to `stdout`).



.. setting:: worker\_pidfile



``worker\_pidfile``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional file path for :program:`celery worker` to create/store its PID file (defaults to no PID file created).



.. setting:: worker\_uid



``worker\_uid``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional user ID to use when :program:`celery worker` daemon drops its privileges (defaults to no UID change).



.. setting:: worker\_gid



``worker\_gid``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional group ID to use when :program:`celery worker` daemon drops its privileges (defaults to no GID change).



.. setting:: worker\_umask



``worker\_umask``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional `umask` to use when :program:`celery worker` creates files (log, pid...) when daemonizing.



.. setting:: worker\_executable



``worker\_executable``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional `python` executable path for :program:`celery worker` to use when deaemonizing (defaults to :data:`sys.executable`).



.. \_conf-celerybeat:



Beat Settings (:program:`celery beat`)

\--------------------------------------



.. setting:: beat\_schedule



``beat\_schedule``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: ``{}`` (empty mapping).



The periodic task schedule used by :mod:`\~celery.bin.beat`.

See :ref:`beat-entries`.



.. setting:: beat\_scheduler



``beat\_scheduler``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: ``"celery.beat:PersistentScheduler"``.



The default scheduler class. May be set to

``"django\_celery\_beat.schedulers:DatabaseScheduler"`` for instance,

if used alongside :pypi:`django-celery-beat` extension.



Can also be set via the :option:`celery beat -S` argument.



.. setting:: beat\_schedule\_filename



``beat\_schedule\_filename``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: ``"celerybeat-schedule"``.



Name of the file used by `PersistentScheduler` to store the last run times

of periodic tasks. Can be a relative or absolute path, but be aware that the

suffix `.db` may be appended to the file name (depending on Python version).



Can also be set via the :option:`celery beat --schedule` argument.



.. setting:: beat\_sync\_every



``beat\_sync\_every``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: 0.



The number of periodic tasks that can be called before another database sync

is issued.

A value of 0 (default) means sync based on timing - default of 3 minutes as determined by

scheduler.sync\_every. If set to 1, beat will call sync after every task

message sent.



.. setting:: beat\_max\_loop\_interval



``beat\_max\_loop\_interval``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Default: 0.



The maximum number of seconds :mod:`\~celery.bin.beat` can sleep

between checking the schedule.



The default for this value is scheduler specific.

For the default Celery beat scheduler the value is 300 (5 minutes),

but for the :pypi:`django-celery-beat` database scheduler it's 5 seconds

because the schedule may be changed externally, and so it must take

changes to the schedule into account.



Also when running Celery beat embedded (:option:`-B <celery worker -B>`)

on Jython as a thread the max interval is overridden and set to 1 so

that it's possible to shut down in a timely manner.



.. setting:: beat\_cron\_starting\_deadline



``beat\_cron\_starting\_deadline``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.3



Default: None.



When using cron, the number of seconds :mod:`\~celery.bin.beat` can look back

when deciding whether a cron schedule is due. When set to `None`, cronjobs that

are past due will always run immediately.



.. warning::



&#x20;   Setting this higher than 3600 (1 hour) is highly discouraged.



.. setting:: beat\_logfile



``beat\_logfile``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional file path for :program:`celery beat` to log into (defaults to `stdout`).



.. setting:: beat\_pidfile



``beat\_pidfile``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional file path for :program:`celery beat` to create/store it PID file (defaults to no PID file created).



.. setting:: beat\_uid



``beat\_uid``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional user ID to use when beat :program:`celery beat` drops its privileges (defaults to no UID change).



.. setting:: beat\_gid



``beat\_gid``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional group ID to use when :program:`celery beat` daemon drops its privileges (defaults to no GID change).



.. setting:: beat\_umask



``beat\_umask``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional `umask` to use when :program:`celery beat` creates files (log, pid...) when daemonizing.



.. setting:: beat\_executable



``beat\_executable``

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



.. versionadded:: 5.4



Default: :const:`None`



An optional `python` executable path for :program:`celery beat` to use when deaemonizing (defaults to :data:`sys.executable`).



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\daemonizing.rst

```rst

.. \_daemonizing:



======================================================================

&#x20; Daemonization

======================================================================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:



Most Linux distributions these days use systemd for managing the lifecycle of system

and user services.



You can check if your Linux distribution uses systemd by typing:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ systemctl --version

&#x20;   systemd 249 (v249.9-1.fc35)

&#x20;   +PAM +AUDIT +SELINUX -APPARMOR +IMA +SMACK +SECCOMP +GCRYPT +GNUTLS +OPENSSL +ACL +BLKID +CURL +ELFUTILS +FIDO2 +IDN2 -IDN +IPTC +KMOD +LIBCRYPTSETUP +LIBFDISK +PCRE2 +PWQUALITY +P11KIT +QRENCODE +BZIP2 +LZ4 +XZ +ZLIB +ZSTD +XKBCOMMON +UTMP +SYSVINIT default-hierarchy=unified



If you have output similar to the above, please refer to

:ref:`our systemd documentation <daemon-systemd-generic>` for guidance.



However, the init.d script should still work in those Linux distributions

as well since systemd provides the systemd-sysv compatibility layer

which generates services automatically from the init.d scripts we provide.



If you package Celery for multiple Linux distributions

and some do not support systemd or to other Unix systems as well,

you may want to refer to :ref:`our init.d documentation <daemon-generic>`.



.. \_daemon-generic:



Generic init-scripts

======================================================================



See the `extra/generic-init.d/`\_ directory Celery distribution.



This directory contains generic bash init-scripts for the

:program:`celery worker` program,

these should run on Linux, FreeBSD, OpenBSD, and other Unix-like platforms.



.. \_`extra/generic-init.d/`:

&#x20;   https://github.com/celery/celery/tree/main/extra/generic-init.d/



.. \_generic-initd-celeryd:



Init-script: ``celeryd``

\----------------------------------------------------------------------



:Usage: `/etc/init.d/celeryd {start|stop|restart|status}`

:Configuration file: :file:`/etc/default/celeryd`



To configure this script to run the worker properly you probably need to at least

tell it where to change

directory to when it starts (to find the module containing your app, or your

configuration module).



The daemonization script is configured by the file :file:`/etc/default/celeryd`.

This is a shell (:command:`sh`) script where you can add environment variables like

the configuration options below.  To add real environment variables affecting

the worker you must also export them (e.g., :command:`export DISPLAY=":0"`)



.. Admonition:: Superuser privileges required



&#x20;   The init-scripts can only be used by root,

&#x20;   and the shell configuration file must also be owned by root.



&#x20;   Unprivileged users don't need to use the init-script,

&#x20;   instead they can use the :program:`celery multi` utility (or

&#x20;   :program:`celery worker --detach`):



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj multi start worker1 \\

&#x20;           --pidfile="$HOME/run/celery/%n.pid" \\

&#x20;           --logfile="$HOME/log/celery/%n%I.log"



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj multi restart worker1 \\

&#x20;           --logfile="$HOME/log/celery/%n%I.log" \\

&#x20;           --pidfile="$HOME/run/celery/%n.pid



&#x20;       $ celery multi stopwait worker1 --pidfile="$HOME/run/celery/%n.pid"



.. \_generic-initd-celeryd-example:



Example configuration

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



This is an example configuration for a Python project.



:file:`/etc/default/celeryd`:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   # Names of nodes to start

&#x20;   #   most people will only start one node:

&#x20;   CELERYD\_NODES="worker1"

&#x20;   #   but you can also start multiple and configure settings

&#x20;   #   for each in CELERYD\_OPTS

&#x20;   #CELERYD\_NODES="worker1 worker2 worker3"

&#x20;   #   alternatively, you can specify the number of nodes to start:

&#x20;   #CELERYD\_NODES=10



&#x20;   # Absolute or relative path to the 'celery' command:

&#x20;   CELERY\_BIN="/usr/local/bin/celery"

&#x20;   #CELERY\_BIN="/virtualenvs/def/bin/celery"



&#x20;   # App instance to use

&#x20;   # comment out this line if you don't use an app

&#x20;   CELERY\_APP="proj"

&#x20;   # or fully qualified:

&#x20;   #CELERY\_APP="proj.tasks:app"



&#x20;   # Where to chdir at start.

&#x20;   CELERYD\_CHDIR="/opt/Myproject/"



&#x20;   # Extra command-line arguments to the worker

&#x20;   CELERYD\_OPTS="--time-limit=300 --concurrency=8"

&#x20;   # Configure node-specific settings by appending node name to arguments:

&#x20;   #CELERYD\_OPTS="--time-limit=300 -c 8 -c:worker2 4 -c:worker3 2 -Ofair:worker1"



&#x20;   # Set logging level to DEBUG

&#x20;   #CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL="DEBUG"



&#x20;   # %n will be replaced with the first part of the nodename.

&#x20;   CELERYD\_LOG\_FILE="/var/log/celery/%n%I.log"

&#x20;   CELERYD\_PID\_FILE="/var/run/celery/%n.pid"



&#x20;   # Workers should run as an unprivileged user.

&#x20;   #   You need to create this user manually (or you can choose

&#x20;   #   a user/group combination that already exists (e.g., nobody).

&#x20;   CELERYD\_USER="celery"

&#x20;   CELERYD\_GROUP="celery"



&#x20;   # If enabled pid and log directories will be created if missing,

&#x20;   # and owned by the userid/group configured.

&#x20;   CELERY\_CREATE\_DIRS=1



Using a login shell

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



You can inherit the environment of the ``CELERYD\_USER`` by using a login

shell:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   CELERYD\_SU\_ARGS="-l"



Note that this isn't recommended, and that you should only use this option

when absolutely necessary.



.. \_generic-initd-celeryd-django-example:



Example Django configuration

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



Django users now uses the exact same template as above,

but make sure that the module that defines your Celery app instance

also sets a default value for :envvar:`DJANGO\_SETTINGS\_MODULE`

as shown in the example Django project in :ref:`django-first-steps`.



.. \_generic-initd-celeryd-options:



Available options

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



\* ``CELERY\_APP``



&#x20;   App instance to use (value for :option:`--app <celery --app>` argument).



\* ``CELERY\_BIN``



&#x20;   Absolute or relative path to the :program:`celery` program.

&#x20;   Examples:



&#x20;       \* :file:`celery`

&#x20;       \* :file:`/usr/local/bin/celery`

&#x20;       \* :file:`/virtualenvs/proj/bin/celery`

&#x20;       \* :file:`/virtualenvs/proj/bin/python -m celery`



\* ``CELERYD\_NODES``



&#x20;   List of node names to start (separated by space).



\* ``CELERYD\_OPTS``



&#x20;   Additional command-line arguments for the worker, see

&#x20;   `celery worker --help` for a list. This also supports the extended

&#x20;   syntax used by `multi` to configure settings for individual nodes.

&#x20;   See `celery multi --help` for some multi-node configuration examples.



\* ``CELERYD\_CHDIR``



&#x20;   Path to change directory to at start. Default is to stay in the current

&#x20;   directory.



\* ``CELERYD\_PID\_FILE``



&#x20;   Full path to the PID file. Default is /var/run/celery/%n.pid



\* ``CELERYD\_LOG\_FILE``



&#x20;   Full path to the worker log file. Default is /var/log/celery/%n%I.log

&#x20;   \*\*Note\*\*: Using `%I` is important when using the prefork pool as having

&#x20;   multiple processes share the same log file will lead to race conditions.



\* ``CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL``



&#x20;   Worker log level. Default is INFO.



\* ``CELERYD\_USER``



&#x20;   User to run the worker as. Default is current user.



\* ``CELERYD\_GROUP``



&#x20;   Group to run worker as. Default is current user.



\* ``CELERY\_CREATE\_DIRS``



&#x20;   Always create directories (log directory and pid file directory).

&#x20;   Default is to only create directories when no custom logfile/pidfile set.



\* ``CELERY\_CREATE\_RUNDIR``



&#x20;   Always create pidfile directory. By default only enabled when no custom

&#x20;   pidfile location set.



\* ``CELERY\_CREATE\_LOGDIR``



&#x20;   Always create logfile directory. By default only enable when no custom

&#x20;   logfile location set.



.. \_generic-initd-celerybeat:



Init-script: ``celerybeat``

\----------------------------------------------------------------------

:Usage: `/etc/init.d/celerybeat {start|stop|restart}`

:Configuration file: :file:`/etc/default/celerybeat` or

&#x20;                    :file:`/etc/default/celeryd`.



.. \_generic-initd-celerybeat-example:



Example configuration

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



This is an example configuration for a Python project:



`/etc/default/celerybeat`:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   # Absolute or relative path to the 'celery' command:

&#x20;   CELERY\_BIN="/usr/local/bin/celery"

&#x20;   #CELERY\_BIN="/virtualenvs/def/bin/celery"



&#x20;   # App instance to use

&#x20;   # comment out this line if you don't use an app

&#x20;   CELERY\_APP="proj"

&#x20;   # or fully qualified:

&#x20;   #CELERY\_APP="proj.tasks:app"



&#x20;   # Where to chdir at start.

&#x20;   CELERYBEAT\_CHDIR="/opt/Myproject/"



&#x20;   # Extra arguments to celerybeat

&#x20;   CELERYBEAT\_OPTS="--schedule=/var/run/celery/celerybeat-schedule"



.. \_generic-initd-celerybeat-django-example:



Example Django configuration

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



You should use the same template as above, but make sure the

``DJANGO\_SETTINGS\_MODULE`` variable is set (and exported), and that

``CELERYD\_CHDIR`` is set to the projects directory:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   export DJANGO\_SETTINGS\_MODULE="settings"



&#x20;   CELERYD\_CHDIR="/opt/MyProject"

.. \_generic-initd-celerybeat-options:



Available options

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



\* ``CELERY\_APP``



&#x20;   App instance to use (value for :option:`--app <celery --app>` argument).



\* ``CELERYBEAT\_OPTS``



&#x20;   Additional arguments to :program:`celery beat`, see

&#x20;   :command:`celery beat --help` for a list of available options.



\* ``CELERYBEAT\_PID\_FILE``



&#x20;   Full path to the PID file. Default is :file:`/var/run/celeryd.pid`.



\* ``CELERYBEAT\_LOG\_FILE``



&#x20;   Full path to the log file. Default is :file:`/var/log/celeryd.log`.



\* ``CELERYBEAT\_LOG\_LEVEL``



&#x20;   Log level to use. Default is ``INFO``.



\* ``CELERYBEAT\_USER``



&#x20;   User to run beat as. Default is the current user.



\* ``CELERYBEAT\_GROUP``



&#x20;   Group to run beat as. Default is the current user.



\* ``CELERY\_CREATE\_DIRS``



&#x20;   Always create directories (log directory and pid file directory).

&#x20;   Default is to only create directories when no custom logfile/pidfile set.



\* ``CELERY\_CREATE\_RUNDIR``



&#x20;   Always create pidfile directory. By default only enabled when no custom

&#x20;   pidfile location set.



\* ``CELERY\_CREATE\_LOGDIR``



&#x20;   Always create logfile directory. By default only enable when no custom

&#x20;   logfile location set.



.. \_generic-initd-troubleshooting:



Troubleshooting

\----------------------------------------------------------------------



If you can't get the init-scripts to work, you should try running

them in \*verbose mode\*:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   # sh -x /etc/init.d/celeryd start



This can reveal hints as to why the service won't start.



If the worker starts with \*"OK"\* but exits almost immediately afterwards

and there's no evidence in the log file, then there's probably an error

but as the daemons standard outputs are already closed you'll

not be able to see them anywhere. For this situation you can use

the :envvar:`C\_FAKEFORK` environment variable to skip the

daemonization step:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   # C\_FAKEFORK=1 sh -x /etc/init.d/celeryd start





and now you should be able to see the errors.



Commonly such errors are caused by insufficient permissions

to read from, or write to a file, and also by syntax errors

in configuration modules, user modules, third-party libraries,

or even from Celery itself (if you've found a bug you

should :ref:`report it <reporting-bugs>`).





.. \_daemon-systemd-generic:



Usage ``systemd``

======================================================================



\* `extra/systemd/`\_



.. \_`extra/systemd/`:

&#x20;   https://github.com/celery/celery/tree/main/extra/systemd/



.. \_generic-systemd-celery:



:Usage: `systemctl {start|stop|restart|status} celery.service`

:Configuration file: /etc/conf.d/celery



Service file: celery.service

\----------------------------------------------------------------------



This is an example systemd file:



:file:`/etc/systemd/system/celery.service`:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   \[Unit]

&#x20;   Description=Celery Service

&#x20;   After=network.target



&#x20;   \[Service]

&#x20;   Type=forking

&#x20;   User=celery

&#x20;   Group=celery

&#x20;   EnvironmentFile=/etc/conf.d/celery

&#x20;   WorkingDirectory=/opt/celery

&#x20;   ExecStart=/bin/sh -c '${CELERY\_BIN} -A $CELERY\_APP multi start $CELERYD\_NODES \\

&#x20;       --pidfile=${CELERYD\_PID\_FILE} --logfile=${CELERYD\_LOG\_FILE} \\

&#x20;       --loglevel="${CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL}" $CELERYD\_OPTS'

&#x20;   ExecStop=/bin/sh -c '${CELERY\_BIN} multi stopwait $CELERYD\_NODES \\

&#x20;       --pidfile=${CELERYD\_PID\_FILE} --logfile=${CELERYD\_LOG\_FILE} \\

&#x20;       --loglevel="${CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL}"'

&#x20;   ExecReload=/bin/sh -c '${CELERY\_BIN} -A $CELERY\_APP multi restart $CELERYD\_NODES \\

&#x20;       --pidfile=${CELERYD\_PID\_FILE} --logfile=${CELERYD\_LOG\_FILE} \\

&#x20;       --loglevel="${CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL}" $CELERYD\_OPTS'

&#x20;   Restart=always



&#x20;   \[Install]

&#x20;   WantedBy=multi-user.target



Once you've put that file in :file:`/etc/systemd/system`, you should run

:command:`systemctl daemon-reload` in order that Systemd acknowledges that file.

You should also run that command each time you modify it.

Use :command:`systemctl enable celery.service` if you want the celery service to

automatically start when (re)booting the system.



Optionally you can specify extra dependencies for the celery service: e.g. if you use

RabbitMQ as a broker, you could specify ``rabbitmq-server.service`` in both ``After=`` and ``Requires=``

in the ``\[Unit]`` `systemd section <https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd.unit.html#%5BUnit%5D%20Section%20Options>`\_.



To configure user, group, :command:`chdir` change settings:

``User``, ``Group``, and ``WorkingDirectory`` defined in

:file:`/etc/systemd/system/celery.service`.



You can also use systemd-tmpfiles in order to create working directories (for logs and pid).



:file: `/etc/tmpfiles.d/celery.conf`



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20; d /run/celery 0755 celery celery -

&#x20; d /var/log/celery 0755 celery celery -





.. \_generic-systemd-celery-example:



Example configuration

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



This is an example configuration for a Python project:



:file:`/etc/conf.d/celery`:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   # Name of nodes to start

&#x20;   # here we have a single node

&#x20;   CELERYD\_NODES="w1"

&#x20;   # or we could have three nodes:

&#x20;   #CELERYD\_NODES="w1 w2 w3"



&#x20;   # Absolute or relative path to the 'celery' command:

&#x20;   CELERY\_BIN="/usr/local/bin/celery"

&#x20;   #CELERY\_BIN="/virtualenvs/def/bin/celery"



&#x20;   # App instance to use

&#x20;   # comment out this line if you don't use an app

&#x20;   CELERY\_APP="proj"

&#x20;   # or fully qualified:

&#x20;   #CELERY\_APP="proj.tasks:app"



&#x20;   # How to call manage.py

&#x20;   CELERYD\_MULTI="multi"



&#x20;   # Extra command-line arguments to the worker

&#x20;   CELERYD\_OPTS="--time-limit=300 --concurrency=8"



&#x20;   # - %n will be replaced with the first part of the nodename.

&#x20;   # - %I will be replaced with the current child process index

&#x20;   #   and is important when using the prefork pool to avoid race conditions.

&#x20;   CELERYD\_PID\_FILE="/var/run/celery/%n.pid"

&#x20;   CELERYD\_LOG\_FILE="/var/log/celery/%n%I.log"

&#x20;   CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL="INFO"



&#x20;   # you may wish to add these options for Celery Beat

&#x20;   CELERYBEAT\_PID\_FILE="/var/run/celery/beat.pid"

&#x20;   CELERYBEAT\_LOG\_FILE="/var/log/celery/beat.log"



Service file: celerybeat.service

\----------------------------------------------------------------------



This is an example systemd file for Celery Beat:



:file:`/etc/systemd/system/celerybeat.service`:



.. code-block:: bash



&#x20;   \[Unit]

&#x20;   Description=Celery Beat Service

&#x20;   After=network.target



&#x20;   \[Service]

&#x20;   Type=simple

&#x20;   User=celery

&#x20;   Group=celery

&#x20;   EnvironmentFile=/etc/conf.d/celery

&#x20;   WorkingDirectory=/opt/celery

&#x20;   ExecStart=/bin/sh -c '${CELERY\_BIN} -A ${CELERY\_APP} beat  \\

&#x20;       --pidfile=${CELERYBEAT\_PID\_FILE} \\

&#x20;       --logfile=${CELERYBEAT\_LOG\_FILE} --loglevel=${CELERYD\_LOG\_LEVEL}'

&#x20;   Restart=always



&#x20;   \[Install]

&#x20;   WantedBy=multi-user.target



Once you've put that file in :file:`/etc/systemd/system`, you should run

:command:`systemctl daemon-reload` in order that Systemd acknowledges that file.

You should also run that command each time you modify it.

Use :command:`systemctl enable celerybeat.service` if you want the celery beat

service to automatically start when (re)booting the system.



Running the worker with superuser privileges (root)

======================================================================



Running the worker with superuser privileges is a very dangerous practice.

There should always be a workaround to avoid running as root. Celery may

run arbitrary code in messages serialized with pickle - this is dangerous,

especially when run as root.



By default Celery won't run workers as root. The associated error

message may not be visible in the logs but may be seen if :envvar:`C\_FAKEFORK`

is used.



To force Celery to run workers as root use :envvar:`C\_FORCE\_ROOT`.



When running as root without :envvar:`C\_FORCE\_ROOT` the worker will

appear to start with \*"OK"\* but exit immediately after with no apparent

errors. This problem may appear when running the project in a new development

or production environment (inadvertently) as root.



.. \_daemon-supervisord:



:pypi:`supervisor`

======================================================================



\* `extra/supervisord/`\_



.. \_`extra/supervisord/`:

&#x20;   https://github.com/celery/celery/tree/main/extra/supervisord/



.. \_daemon-launchd:



``launchd`` (macOS)

======================================================================



\* `extra/macOS`\_



.. \_`extra/macOS`:

&#x20;   https://github.com/celery/celery/tree/main/extra/macOS/



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\debugging.rst

```rst

.. \_guide-debugging:



======================================

&#x20;Debugging

======================================



.. \_tut-remote\_debug:



Debugging Tasks Remotely (using pdb)

====================================



Basics

\------



:mod:`celery.contrib.rdb` is an extended version of :mod:`pdb` that

enables remote debugging of processes that doesn't have terminal

access.



Example usage:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import task

&#x20;   from celery.contrib import rdb



&#x20;   @task()

&#x20;   def add(x, y):

&#x20;       result = x + y

&#x20;       rdb.set\_trace()  # <- set break-point

&#x20;       return result





:func:`\~celery.contrib.rdb.set\_trace` sets a break-point at the current

location and creates a socket you can telnet into to remotely debug

your task.



The debugger may be started by multiple processes at the same time,

so rather than using a fixed port the debugger will search for an

available port, starting from the base port (6900 by default).

The base port can be changed using the environment variable

:envvar:`CELERY\_RDB\_PORT`.



By default the debugger will only be available from the local host,

to enable access from the outside you have to set the environment

variable :envvar:`CELERY\_RDB\_HOST`.



When the worker encounters your break-point it'll log the following

information:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   \[INFO/MainProcess] Received task:

&#x20;       tasks.add\[d7261c71-4962-47e5-b342-2448bedd20e8]

&#x20;   \[WARNING/PoolWorker-1] Remote Debugger:6900:

&#x20;       Please telnet 127.0.0.1 6900.  Type `exit` in session to continue.

&#x20;   \[2011-01-18 14:25:44,119: WARNING/PoolWorker-1] Remote Debugger:6900:

&#x20;       Waiting for client...



If you telnet the port specified you'll be presented

with a `pdb` shell:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ telnet localhost 6900

&#x20;   Connected to localhost.

&#x20;   Escape character is '^]'.

&#x20;   > /opt/devel/demoapp/tasks.py(128)add()

&#x20;   -> return result

&#x20;   (Pdb)



Enter ``help`` to get a list of available commands,

It may be a good idea to read the `Python Debugger Manual`\_ if

you have never used `pdb` before.



To demonstrate, we'll read the value of the ``result`` variable,

change it and continue execution of the task:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   (Pdb) result

&#x20;   4

&#x20;   (Pdb) result = 'hello from rdb'

&#x20;   (Pdb) continue

&#x20;   Connection closed by foreign host.



The result of our vandalism can be seen in the worker logs:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   \[2011-01-18 14:35:36,599: INFO/MainProcess] Task

&#x20;       tasks.add\[d7261c71-4962-47e5-b342-2448bedd20e8] succeeded

&#x20;       in 61.481s: 'hello from rdb'



.. \_`Python Debugger Manual`: http://docs.python.org/library/pdb.html





Tips

\----



.. \_breakpoint\_signal:



Enabling the break-point signal

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



If the environment variable :envvar:`CELERY\_RDBSIG` is set, the worker

will open up an rdb instance whenever the `SIGUSR2` signal is sent.

This is the case for both main and worker processes.



For example starting the worker with:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ CELERY\_RDBSIG=1 celery worker -l INFO



You can start an rdb session for any of the worker processes by executing:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ kill -USR2 <pid>



```



\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\extending.rst

```rst

.. \_guide-extending:



==========================

&#x20;Extensions and Bootsteps

==========================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:

&#x20;   :depth: 2



.. \_extending-custom-consumers:



Custom Message Consumers

========================



You may want to embed custom Kombu consumers to manually process your messages.



For that purpose a special :class:`\~celery.bootstep.ConsumerStep` bootstep class

exists, where you only need to define the ``get\_consumers`` method, that must

return a list of :class:`kombu.Consumer` objects to start

whenever the connection is established:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery

&#x20;   from celery import bootsteps

&#x20;   from kombu import Consumer, Exchange, Queue



&#x20;   my\_queue = Queue('custom', Exchange('custom'), 'routing\_key')



&#x20;   app = Celery(broker='amqp://')





&#x20;   class MyConsumerStep(bootsteps.ConsumerStep):



&#x20;       def get\_consumers(self, channel):

&#x20;           return \[Consumer(channel,

&#x20;                            queues=\[my\_queue],

&#x20;                            callbacks=\[self.handle\_message],

&#x20;                            accept=\['json'])]



&#x20;       def handle\_message(self, body, message):

&#x20;           print('Received message: {0!r}'.format(body))

&#x20;           message.ack()

&#x20;   app.steps\['consumer'].add(MyConsumerStep)



&#x20;   def send\_me\_a\_message(who, producer=None):

&#x20;       with app.producer\_or\_acquire(producer) as producer:

&#x20;           producer.publish(

&#x20;               {'hello': who},

&#x20;               serializer='json',

&#x20;               exchange=my\_queue.exchange,

&#x20;               routing\_key='routing\_key',

&#x20;               declare=\[my\_queue],

&#x20;               retry=True,

&#x20;           )



&#x20;   if \_\_name\_\_ == '\_\_main\_\_':

&#x20;       send\_me\_a\_message('world!')





.. note::



&#x20;   Kombu Consumers can take use of two different message callback dispatching

&#x20;   mechanisms. The first one is the ``callbacks`` argument that accepts

&#x20;   a list of callbacks with a ``(body, message)`` signature,

&#x20;   the second one is the ``on\_message`` argument that takes a single

&#x20;   callback with a ``(message,)`` signature. The latter won't

&#x20;   automatically decode and deserialize the payload.



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       def get\_consumers(self, channel):

&#x20;           return \[Consumer(channel, queues=\[my\_queue],

&#x20;                            on\_message=self.on\_message)]





&#x20;       def on\_message(self, message):

&#x20;           payload = message.decode()

&#x20;           print(

&#x20;               'Received message: {0!r} {props!r} rawlen={s}'.format(

&#x20;               payload, props=message.properties, s=len(message.body),

&#x20;           ))

&#x20;           message.ack()



.. \_extending-blueprints:



Blueprints

==========



Bootsteps is a technique to add functionality to the workers.

A bootstep is a custom class that defines hooks to do custom actions

at different stages in the worker. Every bootstep belongs to a blueprint,

and the worker currently defines two blueprints: \*\*Worker\*\*, and \*\*Consumer\*\*



\----------------------------------------------------------



\*\*Figure A:\*\* Bootsteps in the Worker and Consumer blueprints. Starting

&#x20;             from the bottom up the first step in the worker blueprint

&#x20;             is the Timer, and the last step is to start the Consumer blueprint,

&#x20;             that then establishes the broker connection and starts

&#x20;             consuming messages.



.. figure:: ../images/worker\_graph\_full.png



\----------------------------------------------------------



.. \_extending-worker\_blueprint:



Worker

======



The Worker is the first blueprint to start, and with it starts major components like

the event loop, processing pool, and the timer used for ETA tasks and other

timed events.



When the worker is fully started it continues with the Consumer blueprint,

that sets up how tasks are executed, connects to the broker and starts

the message consumers.



The :class:`\~celery.worker.WorkController` is the core worker implementation,

and contains several methods and attributes that you can use in your bootstep.



.. \_extending-worker\_blueprint-attributes:



Attributes

\----------



.. \_extending-worker-app:



.. attribute:: app



&#x20;   The current app instance.



.. \_extending-worker-hostname:



.. attribute:: hostname



&#x20;   The workers node name (e.g., `worker1@example.com`)



.. \_extending-worker-blueprint:



.. attribute:: blueprint



&#x20;   This is the worker :class:`\~celery.bootsteps.Blueprint`.



.. \_extending-worker-hub:



.. attribute:: hub



&#x20;   Event loop object (:class:`\~kombu.asynchronous.Hub`). You can use

&#x20;   this to register callbacks in the event loop.



&#x20;   This is only supported by async I/O enabled transports (amqp, redis),

&#x20;   in which case the `worker.use\_eventloop` attribute should be set.



&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the Hub bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.components:Hub'}



.. \_extending-worker-pool:



.. attribute:: pool



&#x20;   The current process/eventlet/gevent/thread pool.

&#x20;   See :class:`celery.concurrency.base.BasePool`.



&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the Pool bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.components:Pool'}



.. \_extending-worker-timer:



.. attribute:: timer



&#x20;   :class:`\~kombu.asynchronous.timer.Timer` used to schedule functions.



&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the Timer bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.components:Timer'}



.. \_extending-worker-statedb:



.. attribute:: statedb



&#x20;   :class:`Database <celery.worker.state.Persistent>`` to persist state between

&#x20;   worker restarts.



&#x20;   This is only defined if the ``statedb`` argument is enabled.



&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the ``Statedb`` bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.components:Statedb'}



.. \_extending-worker-autoscaler:



.. attribute:: autoscaler



&#x20;   :class:`\~celery.worker.autoscaler.Autoscaler` used to automatically grow

&#x20;   and shrink the number of processes in the pool.



&#x20;   This is only defined if the ``autoscale`` argument is enabled.



&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the `Autoscaler` bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = ('celery.worker.autoscaler:Autoscaler',)



.. \_extending-worker-autoreloader:



.. attribute:: autoreloader



&#x20;   :class:`\~celery.worker.autoreloder.Autoreloader` used to automatically

&#x20;   reload use code when the file-system changes.



&#x20;   This is only defined if the ``autoreload`` argument is enabled.

&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the `Autoreloader` bootstep to use this;



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = ('celery.worker.autoreloader:Autoreloader',)



Example worker bootstep

\-----------------------



An example Worker bootstep could be:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import bootsteps



&#x20;   class ExampleWorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;       requires = {'celery.worker.components:Pool'}



&#x20;       def \_\_init\_\_(self, worker, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;           print('Called when the WorkController instance is constructed')

&#x20;           print('Arguments to WorkController: {0!r}'.format(kwargs))



&#x20;       def create(self, worker):

&#x20;           # this method can be used to delegate the action methods

&#x20;           # to another object that implements ``start`` and ``stop``.

&#x20;           return self



&#x20;       def start(self, worker):

&#x20;           print('Called when the worker is started.')



&#x20;       def stop(self, worker):

&#x20;           print('Called when the worker shuts down.')



&#x20;       def terminate(self, worker):

&#x20;           print('Called when the worker terminates')





Every method is passed the current ``WorkController`` instance as the first

argument.



Another example could use the timer to wake up at regular intervals:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import bootsteps





&#x20;   class DeadlockDetection(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;       requires = {'celery.worker.components:Timer'}



&#x20;       def \_\_init\_\_(self, worker, deadlock\_timeout=3600):

&#x20;           self.timeout = deadlock\_timeout

&#x20;           self.requests = \[]

&#x20;           self.tref = None



&#x20;       def start(self, worker):

&#x20;           # run every 30 seconds.

&#x20;           self.tref = worker.timer.call\_repeatedly(

&#x20;               30.0, self.detect, (worker,), priority=10,

&#x20;           )



&#x20;       def stop(self, worker):

&#x20;           if self.tref:

&#x20;               self.tref.cancel()

&#x20;               self.tref = None



&#x20;       def detect(self, worker):

&#x20;           # update active requests

&#x20;           for req in worker.active\_requests:

&#x20;               if req.time\_start and time() - req.time\_start > self.timeout:

&#x20;                   raise SystemExit()



Customizing Task Handling Logs

\------------------------------



The Celery worker emits messages to the Python logging subsystem for various

events throughout the lifecycle of a task.

These messages can be customized by overriding the ``LOG\_<TYPE>`` format

strings which are defined in :file:`celery/app/trace.py`.

For example:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   import celery.app.trace



&#x20;   celery.app.trace.LOG\_SUCCESS = "This is a custom message"



The various format strings are all provided with the task name and ID for

``%`` formatting, and some of them receive extra fields like the return value

or the exception which caused a task to fail.

These fields can be used in custom format strings like so:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   import celery.app.trace



&#x20;   celery.app.trace.LOG\_REJECTED = "%(name)r is cursed and I won't run it: %(exc)s"



.. \_extending-consumer\_blueprint:



Consumer

========



The Consumer blueprint establishes a connection to the broker, and

is restarted every time this connection is lost. Consumer bootsteps

include the worker heartbeat, the remote control command consumer, and

importantly, the task consumer.



When you create consumer bootsteps you must take into account that it must

be possible to restart your blueprint. An additional 'shutdown' method is

defined for consumer bootsteps, this method is called when the worker is

shutdown.



.. \_extending-consumer-attributes:



Attributes

\----------



.. \_extending-consumer-app:



.. attribute:: app



&#x20;   The current app instance.



.. \_extending-consumer-controller:



.. attribute:: controller



&#x20;   The parent :class:`\~@WorkController` object that created this consumer.



.. \_extending-consumer-hostname:



.. attribute:: hostname



&#x20;   The workers node name (e.g., `worker1@example.com`)



.. \_extending-consumer-blueprint:



.. attribute:: blueprint



&#x20;   This is the worker :class:`\~celery.bootsteps.Blueprint`.



.. \_extending-consumer-hub:



.. attribute:: hub



&#x20;   Event loop object (:class:`\~kombu.asynchronous.Hub`). You can use

&#x20;   this to register callbacks in the event loop.



&#x20;   This is only supported by async I/O enabled transports (amqp, redis),

&#x20;   in which case the `worker.use\_eventloop` attribute should be set.



&#x20;   Your worker bootstep must require the Hub bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class WorkerStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.components:Hub'}



.. \_extending-consumer-connection:



.. attribute:: connection



&#x20;   The current broker connection (:class:`kombu.Connection`).



&#x20;   A consumer bootstep must require the 'Connection' bootstep

&#x20;   to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class Step(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.consumer.connection:Connection'}



.. \_extending-consumer-event\_dispatcher:



.. attribute:: event\_dispatcher



&#x20;   A :class:`@events.Dispatcher` object that can be used to send events.



&#x20;   A consumer bootstep must require the `Events` bootstep to use this.



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class Step(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.consumer.events:Events'}



.. \_extending-consumer-gossip:



.. attribute:: gossip



&#x20;   Worker to worker broadcast communication

&#x20;   (:class:`\~celery.worker.consumer.gossip.Gossip`).



&#x20;   A consumer bootstep must require the `Gossip` bootstep to use this.



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class RatelimitStep(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           """Rate limit tasks based on the number of workers in the

&#x20;           cluster."""

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.consumer.gossip:Gossip'}



&#x20;           def start(self, c):

&#x20;               self.c = c

&#x20;               self.c.gossip.on.node\_join.add(self.on\_cluster\_size\_change)

&#x20;               self.c.gossip.on.node\_leave.add(self.on\_cluster\_size\_change)

&#x20;               self.c.gossip.on.node\_lost.add(self.on\_node\_lost)

&#x20;               self.tasks = \[

&#x20;                   self.app.tasks\['proj.tasks.add']

&#x20;                   self.app.tasks\['proj.tasks.mul']

&#x20;               ]

&#x20;               self.last\_size = None



&#x20;           def on\_cluster\_size\_change(self, worker):

&#x20;               cluster\_size = len(list(self.c.gossip.state.alive\_workers()))

&#x20;               if cluster\_size != self.last\_size:

&#x20;                   for task in self.tasks:

&#x20;                       task.rate\_limit = 1.0 / cluster\_size

&#x20;                   self.c.reset\_rate\_limits()

&#x20;                   self.last\_size = cluster\_size



&#x20;           def on\_node\_lost(self, worker):

&#x20;               # may have processed heartbeat too late, so wake up soon

&#x20;               # in order to see if the worker recovered.

&#x20;               self.c.timer.call\_after(10.0, self.on\_cluster\_size\_change)



&#x20;   \*\*Callbacks\*\*



&#x20;   - ``<set> gossip.on.node\_join``



&#x20;       Called whenever a new node joins the cluster, providing a

&#x20;       :class:`\~celery.events.state.Worker` instance.



&#x20;   - ``<set> gossip.on.node\_leave``



&#x20;       Called whenever a new node leaves the cluster (shuts down),

&#x20;       providing a :class:`\~celery.events.state.Worker` instance.



&#x20;   - ``<set> gossip.on.node\_lost``



&#x20;       Called whenever heartbeat was missed for a worker instance in the

&#x20;       cluster (heartbeat not received or processed in time),

&#x20;       providing a :class:`\~celery.events.state.Worker` instance.



&#x20;       This doesn't necessarily mean the worker is actually offline, so use a time

&#x20;       out mechanism if the default heartbeat timeout isn't sufficient.



.. \_extending-consumer-pool:



.. attribute:: pool



&#x20;   The current process/eventlet/gevent/thread pool.

&#x20;   See :class:`celery.concurrency.base.BasePool`.



.. \_extending-consumer-timer:



.. attribute:: timer



&#x20;   :class:`Timer <celery.utils.timer2.Schedule` used to schedule functions.



.. \_extending-consumer-heart:



.. attribute:: heart



&#x20;   Responsible for sending worker event heartbeats

&#x20;   (:class:`\~celery.worker.heartbeat.Heart`).



&#x20;   Your consumer bootstep must require the `Heart` bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class Step(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.consumer.heart:Heart'}



.. \_extending-consumer-task\_consumer:



.. attribute:: task\_consumer



&#x20;   The :class:`kombu.Consumer` object used to consume task messages.



&#x20;   Your consumer bootstep must require the `Tasks` bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class Step(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.consumer.tasks:Tasks'}



.. \_extending-consumer-strategies:



.. attribute:: strategies



&#x20;   Every registered task type has an entry in this mapping,

&#x20;   where the value is used to execute an incoming message of this task type

&#x20;   (the task execution strategy). This mapping is generated by the Tasks

&#x20;   bootstep when the consumer starts:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       for name, task in app.tasks.items():

&#x20;           strategies\[name] = task.start\_strategy(app, consumer)

&#x20;           task.\_\_trace\_\_ = celery.app.trace.build\_tracer(

&#x20;               name, task, loader, hostname

&#x20;           )



&#x20;   Your consumer bootstep must require the `Tasks` bootstep to use this:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       class Step(bootsteps.StartStopStep):

&#x20;           requires = {'celery.worker.consumer.tasks:Tasks'}



.. \_extending-consumer-task\_buckets:



.. attribute:: task\_buckets



&#x20;   A :class:`\~collections.defaultdict` used to look-up the rate limit for

&#x20;   a task by type.

&#x20;   Entries in this dict may be None (for no limit) or a

&#x20;   :class:`\~kombu.utils.limits.TokenBucket` instance implementing

&#x20;   ``consume(tokens)`` and ``expected\_time(tokens)``.



&#x20;   TokenBucket implements the `token bucket algorithm`\_, but any algorithm

&#x20;   may be used as long as it conforms to the same interface and defines the

&#x20;   two methods above.



&#x20;   .. \_`token bucket algorithm`: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Token\_bucket



.. \_extending\_consumer-qos:



.. attribute:: qos



&#x20;   The :class:`\~kombu.common.QoS` object can be used to change the

&#x20;   task channels current prefetch\_count value:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: python



&#x20;       # increment at next cycle

&#x20;       consumer.qos.increment\_eventually(1)

&#x20;       # decrement at next cycle

&#x20;       consumer.qos.decrement\_eventually(1)

&#x20;       consumer.qos.set(10)





Methods

\-------



.. method:: consumer.reset\_rate\_limits()



&#x20;   Updates the ``task\_buckets`` mapping for all registered task types.



.. method:: consumer.bucket\_for\_task(type, Bucket=TokenBucket)



&#x20;   Creates rate limit bucket for a task using its ``task.rate\_limit``

&#x20;   attribute.



.. method:: consumer.add\_task\_queue(name, exchange=None, exchange\_type=None,

&#x20;                                   routing\_key=None, \\\*\\\*options):



&#x20;   Adds new queue to consume from. This will persist on connection restart.



.. method:: consumer.cancel\_task\_queue(name)



&#x20;   Stop consuming from queue by name. This will persist on connection

&#x20;   restart.



.. method:: apply\_eta\_task(request)



&#x20;   Schedule ETA task to execute based on the ``request.eta`` attribute.

&#x20;   (:class:`\~celery.worker.request.Request`)







.. \_extending-bootsteps:



Installing Bootsteps

====================



``app.steps\['worker']`` and ``app.steps\['consumer']`` can be modified

to add new bootsteps:



.. code-block:: pycon



&#x20;   >>> app = Celery()

&#x20;   >>> app.steps\['worker'].add(MyWorkerStep)  # < add class, don't instantiate

&#x20;   >>> app.steps\['consumer'].add(MyConsumerStep)



&#x20;   >>> app.steps\['consumer'].update(\[StepA, StepB])



&#x20;   >>> app.steps\['consumer']

&#x20;   {step:proj.StepB{()}, step:proj.MyConsumerStep{()}, step:proj.StepA{()}



The order of steps isn't important here as the order is decided by the

resulting dependency graph (``Step.requires``).



To illustrate how you can install bootsteps and how they work, this is an example step that

prints some useless debugging information.

It can be added both as a worker and consumer bootstep:





.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery

&#x20;   from celery import bootsteps



&#x20;   class InfoStep(bootsteps.Step):



&#x20;       def \_\_init\_\_(self, parent, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;           # here we can prepare the Worker/Consumer object

&#x20;           # in any way we want, set attribute defaults, and so on.

&#x20;           print('{0!r} is in init'.format(parent))



&#x20;       def start(self, parent):

&#x20;           # our step is started together with all other Worker/Consumer

&#x20;           # bootsteps.

&#x20;           print('{0!r} is starting'.format(parent))



&#x20;       def stop(self, parent):

&#x20;           # the Consumer calls stop every time the consumer is

&#x20;           # restarted (i.e., connection is lost) and also at shutdown.

&#x20;           # The Worker will call stop at shutdown only.

&#x20;           print('{0!r} is stopping'.format(parent))



&#x20;       def shutdown(self, parent):

&#x20;           # shutdown is called by the Consumer at shutdown, it's not

&#x20;           # called by Worker.

&#x20;           print('{0!r} is shutting down'.format(parent))



&#x20;       app = Celery(broker='amqp://')

&#x20;       app.steps\['worker'].add(InfoStep)

&#x20;       app.steps\['consumer'].add(InfoStep)



Starting the worker with this step installed will give us the following

logs:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   <Worker: w@example.com (initializing)> is in init

&#x20;   <Consumer: w@example.com (initializing)> is in init

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,544: WARNING/MainProcess]

&#x20;       <Worker: w@example.com (running)> is starting

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,577: WARNING/MainProcess]

&#x20;       <Consumer: w@example.com (running)> is starting

&#x20;   <Consumer: w@example.com (closing)> is stopping

&#x20;   <Worker: w@example.com (closing)> is stopping

&#x20;   <Consumer: w@example.com (terminating)> is shutting down



The ``print`` statements will be redirected to the logging subsystem after

the worker has been initialized, so the "is starting" lines are time-stamped.

You may notice that this does no longer happen at shutdown, this is because

the ``stop`` and ``shutdown`` methods are called inside a \*signal handler\*,

and it's not safe to use logging inside such a handler.

Logging with the Python logging module isn't :term:`reentrant`:

meaning you cannot interrupt the function then

call it again later. It's important that the ``stop`` and ``shutdown`` methods

you write is also :term:`reentrant`.



Starting the worker with :option:`--loglevel=debug <celery worker --loglevel>`

will show us more information about the boot process:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,509: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: Preparing bootsteps.

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,511: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: Building graph...

&#x20;   <celery.apps.worker.Worker object at 0x101ad8410> is in init

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,511: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: New boot order:

&#x20;       {Hub, Pool, Timer, StateDB, Autoscaler, InfoStep, Beat, Consumer}

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,514: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Preparing bootsteps.

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,514: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Building graph...

&#x20;   <celery.worker.consumer.Consumer object at 0x101c2d8d0> is in init

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,515: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: New boot order:

&#x20;       {Connection, Mingle, Events, Gossip, InfoStep, Agent,

&#x20;        Heart, Control, Tasks, event loop}

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,522: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: Starting Hub

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,522: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,522: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: Starting Pool

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,542: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,543: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: Starting InfoStep

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,544: WARNING/MainProcess]

&#x20;       <celery.apps.worker.Worker object at 0x101ad8410> is starting

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,544: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,544: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Worker: Starting Consumer

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,544: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Connection

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,559: INFO/MainProcess] Connected to amqp://guest@127.0.0.1:5672//

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,560: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,560: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Mingle

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:20,560: INFO/MainProcess] mingle: searching for neighbors

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,570: INFO/MainProcess] mingle: no one here

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,570: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,571: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Events

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,572: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,572: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Gossip

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,577: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,577: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting InfoStep

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,577: WARNING/MainProcess]

&#x20;       <celery.worker.consumer.Consumer object at 0x101c2d8d0> is starting

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,578: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,578: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Heart

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,579: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,579: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Control

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,583: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,583: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting Tasks

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,606: DEBUG/MainProcess] basic.qos: prefetch\_count->80

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,606: DEBUG/MainProcess] ^-- substep ok

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,606: DEBUG/MainProcess] | Consumer: Starting event loop

&#x20;   \[2013-05-29 16:18:21,608: WARNING/MainProcess] celery@example.com ready.





.. \_extending-programs:



Command-line programs

=====================



.. \_extending-commandoptions:



Adding new command-line options

\-------------------------------



.. \_extending-command-options:



Command-specific options

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



You can add additional command-line options to the ``worker``, ``beat``, and

``events`` commands by modifying the :attr:`\~@user\_options` attribute of the

application instance.



Celery commands uses the :mod:`click` module to parse command-line

arguments, and so to add custom arguments you need to add :class:`click.Option` instances

to the relevant set.



Example adding a custom option to the :program:`celery worker` command:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery

&#x20;   from click import Option



&#x20;   app = Celery(broker='amqp://')



&#x20;   app.user\_options\['worker'].add(Option(('--enable-my-option',),

&#x20;                                         is\_flag=True,

&#x20;                                         help='Enable custom option.'))





All bootsteps will now receive this argument as a keyword argument to

``Bootstep.\_\_init\_\_``:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import bootsteps



&#x20;   class MyBootstep(bootsteps.Step):



&#x20;       def \_\_init\_\_(self, parent, enable\_my\_option=False, \*\*options):

&#x20;           super().\_\_init\_\_(parent, \*\*options)

&#x20;           if enable\_my\_option:

&#x20;               party()



&#x20;   app.steps\['worker'].add(MyBootstep)



.. \_extending-preload\_options:



Preload options

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



The :program:`celery` umbrella command supports the concept of 'preload

options'.  These are special options passed to all sub-commands.



You can add new preload options, for example to specify a configuration

template:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   from celery import Celery

&#x20;   from celery import signals

&#x20;   from click import Option



&#x20;   app = Celery()



&#x20;   app.user\_options\['preload'].add(Option(('-Z', '--template'),

&#x20;                                          default='default',

&#x20;                                          help='Configuration template to use.'))



&#x20;   @signals.user\_preload\_options.connect

&#x20;   def on\_preload\_parsed(options, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;       use\_template(options\['template'])



.. \_extending-subcommands:



Adding new :program:`celery` sub-commands

\-----------------------------------------



New commands can be added to the :program:`celery` umbrella command by using

`setuptools entry-points`\_.



.. \_`setuptools entry-points`:

&#x20;   http://reinout.vanrees.org/weblog/2010/01/06/zest-releaser-entry-points.html





Entry-points is special meta-data that can be added to your packages ``setup.py`` program,

and then after installation, read from the system using the :mod:`importlib` module.



Celery recognizes ``celery.commands`` entry-points to install additional

sub-commands, where the value of the entry-point must point to a valid click

command.



This is how the :pypi:`Flower` monitoring extension may add the :program:`celery flower` command,

by adding an entry-point in :file:`setup.py`:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   setup(

&#x20;       name='flower',

&#x20;       entry\_points={

&#x20;           'celery.commands': \[

&#x20;              'flower = flower.command:flower',

&#x20;           ],

&#x20;       }

&#x20;   )



The command definition is in two parts separated by the equal sign, where the

first part is the name of the sub-command (flower), then the second part is

the fully qualified symbol path to the function that implements the command:



.. code-block:: text



&#x20;   flower.command:flower



The module path and the name of the attribute should be separated by colon

as above.





In the module :file:`flower/command.py`, the command function may be defined

as the following:



.. code-block:: python



&#x20;   import click



&#x20;   @click.command()

&#x20;   @click.option('--port', default=8888, type=int, help='Webserver port')

&#x20;   @click.option('--debug', is\_flag=True)

&#x20;   def flower(port, debug):

&#x20;       print('Running our command')





Worker API

==========





:class:`\~kombu.asynchronous.Hub` - The workers async event loop

\---------------------------------------------------------------

:supported transports: amqp, redis



.. versionadded:: 3.0



The worker uses asynchronous I/O when the amqp or redis broker transports are

used. The eventual goal is for all transports to use the event-loop, but that

will take some time so other transports still use a threading-based solution.



.. method:: hub.add(fd, callback, flags)





.. method:: hub.add\_reader(fd, callback, \\\*args)



&#x20;   Add callback to be called when ``fd`` is readable.



&#x20;   The callback will stay registered until explicitly removed using

&#x20;   :meth:`hub.remove(fd) <hub.remove>`, or the file descriptor is

&#x20;   automatically discarded because it's no longer valid.



&#x20;   Note that only one callback can be registered for any given

&#x20;   file descriptor at a time, so calling ``add`` a second time will remove

&#x20;   any callback that was previously registered for that file descriptor.



&#x20;   A file descriptor is any file-like object that supports the ``fileno``

&#x20;   method, or it can be the file descriptor number (int).



.. method:: hub.add\_writer(fd, callback, \\\*args)



&#x20;   Add callback to be called when ``fd`` is writable.

&#x20;   See also notes for :meth:`hub.add\_reader` above.



.. method:: hub.remove(fd)



&#x20;   Remove all callbacks for file descriptor ``fd`` from the loop.



Timer - Scheduling events

\-------------------------



.. method:: timer.call\_after(secs, callback, args=(), kwargs=(),

&#x20;                            priority=0)



.. method:: timer.call\_repeatedly(secs, callback, args=(), kwargs=(),

&#x20;                                 priority=0)



.. method:: timer.call\_at(eta, callback, args=(), kwargs=(),

&#x20;                         priority=0)



```

\### 代码文件: docs\\userguide\\monitoring.rst

```rst

.. \_guide-monitoring:



=================================

&#x20;Monitoring and Management Guide

=================================



.. contents::

&#x20;   :local:



Introduction

============



There are several tools available to monitor and inspect Celery clusters.



This document describes some of these, as well as

features related to monitoring, like events and broadcast commands.



.. \_monitoring-workers:



Workers

=======



.. \_monitoring-control:



Management Command-line Utilities (``inspect``/``control``)

\-----------------------------------------------------------





:program:`celery` can also be used to inspect

and manage worker nodes (and to some degree tasks).



To list all the commands available do:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery --help



or to get help for a specific command do:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery <command> --help



Commands

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



\* \*\*shell\*\*: Drop into a Python shell.



&#x20; The locals will include the ``celery`` variable: this is the current app.

&#x20; Also all known tasks will be automatically added to locals (unless the

&#x20; :option:`--without-tasks <celery shell --without-tasks>` flag is set).



&#x20; Uses :pypi:`Ipython`, :pypi:`bpython`, or regular :program:`python` in that

&#x20; order if installed. You can force an implementation using

&#x20; :option:`--ipython <celery shell --ipython>`,

&#x20; :option:`--bpython <celery shell --bpython>`, or

&#x20; :option:`--python <celery shell --python>`.



\* \*\*status\*\*: List active nodes in this cluster



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;           $ celery -A proj status



\* \*\*result\*\*: Show the result of a task



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj result -t tasks.add 4e196aa4-0141-4601-8138-7aa33db0f577



&#x20;   Note that you can omit the name of the task as long as the

&#x20;   task doesn't use a custom result backend.



\* \*\*purge\*\*: Purge messages from all configured task queues.



&#x20;   This command will remove all messages from queues configured in

&#x20;   the :setting:`CELERY\_QUEUES` setting:



&#x20;   .. warning::



&#x20;       There's no undo for this operation, and messages will

&#x20;       be permanently deleted!



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj purge





&#x20;   You can also specify the queues to purge using the `-Q` option:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj purge -Q celery,foo,bar



&#x20;   and exclude queues from being purged using the `-X` option:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj purge -X celery



\* \*\*inspect active\*\*: List active tasks



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect active



&#x20;   These are all the tasks that are currently being executed.



\* \*\*inspect scheduled\*\*: List scheduled ETA tasks



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect scheduled



&#x20;   These are tasks reserved by the worker when they have an

&#x20;   `eta` or `countdown` argument set.



\* \*\*inspect reserved\*\*: List reserved tasks



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect reserved



&#x20;   This will list all tasks that have been prefetched by the worker,

&#x20;   and is currently waiting to be executed (doesn't include tasks

&#x20;   with an ETA value set).



\* \*\*inspect revoked\*\*: List history of revoked tasks



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect revoked



\* \*\*inspect registered\*\*: List registered tasks



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect registered



\* \*\*inspect stats\*\*: Show worker statistics (see :ref:`worker-statistics`)



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect stats



\* \*\*inspect query\_task\*\*: Show information about task(s) by id.



&#x20;   Any worker having a task in this set of ids reserved/active will respond

&#x20;   with status and information.



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect query\_task e9f6c8f0-fec9-4ae8-a8c6-cf8c8451d4f8



&#x20;   You can also query for information about multiple tasks:



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj inspect query\_task id1 id2 ... idN



\* \*\*control enable\_events\*\*: Enable events



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj control enable\_events



\* \*\*control disable\_events\*\*: Disable events



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj control disable\_events



\* \*\*migrate\*\*: Migrate tasks from one broker to another (\*\*EXPERIMENTAL\*\*).



&#x20;   .. code-block:: console



&#x20;       $ celery -A proj migrate redis://localhost amqp://localhost



&#x20; This command will migrate all the tasks on one broker to another.

&#x20; As this command is new and experimental you should be sure to have

&#x20; a backup of the data before proceeding.



.. note::



&#x20;   All ``inspect`` and ``control`` commands supports a

&#x20;   :option:`--timeout <celery inspect --timeout>` argument,

&#x20;   This is the number of seconds to wait for responses.

&#x20;   You may have to increase this timeout if you're not getting a response

&#x20;   due to latency.



.. \_inspect-destination:



Specifying destination nodes

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



By default the inspect and control commands operates on all workers.

You can specify a single, or a list of workers by using the

:option:`--destination <celery inspect --destination>` argument:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj inspect -d w1@e.com,w2@e.com reserved



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj control -d w1@e.com,w2@e.com enable\_events





.. \_monitoring-flower:



Flower: Real-time Celery web-monitor

\------------------------------------



Flower is a real-time web based monitor and administration tool for Celery.

It's under active development, but is already an essential tool.

Being the recommended monitor for Celery, it obsoletes the Django-Admin

monitor, ``celerymon`` and the ``ncurses`` based monitor.



Flower is pronounced like "flow", but you can also use the botanical version

if you prefer.



Features

\~\~\~\~\~\~\~\~



\- Real-time monitoring using Celery Events



&#x20;   - Task progress and history

&#x20;   - Ability to show task details (arguments, start time, run-time, and more)

&#x20;   - Graphs and statistics



\- Remote Control



&#x20;   - View worker status and statistics

&#x20;   - Shutdown and restart worker instances

&#x20;   - Control worker pool size and autoscale settings

&#x20;   - View and modify the queues a worker instance consumes from

&#x20;   - View currently running tasks

&#x20;   - View scheduled tasks (ETA/countdown)

&#x20;   - View reserved and revoked tasks

&#x20;   - Apply time and rate limits

&#x20;   - Configuration viewer

&#x20;   - Revoke or terminate tasks



\- HTTP API



&#x20;   - List workers

&#x20;   - Shut down a worker

&#x20;   - Restart worker’s pool

&#x20;   - Grow worker’s pool

&#x20;   - Shrink worker’s pool

&#x20;   - Autoscale worker pool

&#x20;   - Start consuming from a queue

&#x20;   - Stop consuming from a queue

&#x20;   - List tasks

&#x20;   - List (seen) task types

&#x20;   - Get a task info

&#x20;   - Execute a task

&#x20;   - Execute a task by name

&#x20;   - Get a task result

&#x20;   - Change soft and hard time limits for a task

&#x20;   - Change rate limit for a task

&#x20;   - Revoke a task



\- OpenID authentication



\*\*Screenshots\*\*



.. figure:: ../images/dashboard.png

&#x20;  :width: 700px



More screenshots\_:



.. \_screenshots: https://github.com/mher/flower/tree/master/docs/screenshots



Usage

\~\~\~\~\~



You can use pip to install Flower:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ pip install flower



Running the flower command will start a web-server that you can visit:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj flower



The default port is http://localhost:5555, but you can change this using the

`--port`\_ argument:



.. \_--port: https://flower.readthedocs.io/en/latest/config.html#port



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj flower --port=5555



Broker URL can also be passed through the

:option:`--broker <celery --broker>` argument :



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery --broker=amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672// flower

&#x20;   or

&#x20;   $ celery --broker=redis://guest:guest@localhost:6379/0 flower



Then, you can visit flower in your web browser :



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ open http://localhost:5555



Flower has many more features than are detailed here, including

authorization options. Check out the `official documentation`\_ for more

information.



.. \_official documentation: https://flower.readthedocs.io/en/latest/





.. \_monitoring-celeryev:



celery events: Curses Monitor

\-----------------------------



.. versionadded:: 2.0



`celery events` is a simple curses monitor displaying

task and worker history. You can inspect the result and traceback of tasks,

and it also supports some management commands like rate limiting and shutting

down workers. This monitor was started as a proof of concept, and you

probably want to use Flower instead.



Starting:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj events



You should see a screen like:



.. figure:: ../images/celeryevshotsm.jpg





`celery events` is also used to start snapshot cameras (see

:ref:`monitoring-snapshots`:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj events --camera=<camera-class> --frequency=1.0



and it includes a tool to dump events to :file:`stdout`:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery -A proj events --dump



For a complete list of options use :option:`!--help`:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ celery events --help



.. \_`celerymon`: https://github.com/celery/celerymon/



.. \_monitoring-rabbitmq:



RabbitMQ

========



To manage a Celery cluster it is important to know how

RabbitMQ can be monitored.



RabbitMQ ships with the `rabbitmqctl(1)`\_ command,

with this you can list queues, exchanges, bindings,

queue lengths, the memory usage of each queue, as well

as manage users, virtual hosts and their permissions.



.. note::



&#x20;   The default virtual host (``"/"``) is used in these

&#x20;   examples, if you use a custom virtual host you have to add

&#x20;   the ``-p`` argument to the command, for example:

&#x20;   ``rabbitmqctl list\_queues -p my\_vhost …``



.. \_`rabbitmqctl(1)`: http://www.rabbitmq.com/man/rabbitmqctl.1.man.html



.. \_monitoring-rmq-queues:



Inspecting queues

\-----------------



Finding the number of tasks in a queue:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ rabbitmqctl list\_queues name messages messages\_ready \\

&#x20;                             messages\_unacknowledged





Here `messages\_ready` is the number of messages ready

for delivery (sent but not received), `messages\_unacknowledged`

is the number of messages that's been received by a worker but

not acknowledged yet (meaning it is in progress, or has been reserved).

`messages` is the sum of ready and unacknowledged messages.





Finding the number of workers currently consuming from a queue:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ rabbitmqctl list\_queues name consumers



Finding the amount of memory allocated to a queue:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ rabbitmqctl list\_queues name memory



:Tip: Adding the ``-q`` option to `rabbitmqctl(1)`\_ makes the output

&#x20;     easier to parse.





.. \_monitoring-redis:



Redis

=====



If you're using Redis as the broker, you can monitor the Celery cluster using

the `redis-cli(1)` command to list lengths of queues.



.. \_monitoring-redis-queues:



Inspecting queues

\-----------------



Finding the number of tasks in a queue:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ redis-cli -h HOST -p PORT -n DATABASE\_NUMBER llen QUEUE\_NAME



The default queue is named `celery`. To get all available queues, invoke:



.. code-block:: console



&#x20;   $ redis-cli -h HOST -p PORT -n DATABASE\_NUMBER keys \\\*



.. note::



&#x20;   Queue keys only exists when there are tasks in them, so if a key

&#x20;   doesn't exist it simply means there are no messages in that queue.

&#x20;   This is because in Redis a list with no elements in it is automatically

&#x20;   removed, and hence it won't show up in the `keys` command output,

&#x20;   and `llen` for that list returns 0.



&#x20;   Also, if you're using Redis for other purposes, the

&#x20;   output of the `keys` command will include unrelated values stored in

&#x20;   the database. The recommended way around this is to use a

&#x20;   dedicated `DATABASE\_NUMBER` for Celery, you can also use

&#x20;   database numbers to separate Celery applications from each other (virtual

&#x20;   hosts), but this won't affect the monitoring events used by for example

&#x20;   Flower as Redis pub/sub commands are global rather than database based.



.. \_monitoring-munin:



Munin

=====



This is a list of known Munin plug-ins that can be useful when
```
