"""Threading primitives and utilities."""

import os

import socket

import sys

import threading

import traceback

from contextlib import contextmanager

from threading import TIMEOUT\_MAX as THREAD\_TIMEOUT\_MAX



from celery.local import Proxy



try:

&#x20;   from greenlet import getcurrent as get\_ident

except ImportError:

&#x20;   try:

&#x20;       from \_thread import get\_ident

&#x20;   except ImportError:

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           from thread import get\_ident

&#x20;       except ImportError:

&#x20;           try:

&#x20;               from \_dummy\_thread import get\_ident

&#x20;           except ImportError:

&#x20;               from dummy\_thread import get\_ident





\_\_all\_\_ = (

&#x20;   'bgThread', 'Local', 'LocalStack', 'LocalManager',

&#x20;   'get\_ident', 'default\_socket\_timeout',

)



USE\_FAST\_LOCALS = os.environ.get('USE\_FAST\_LOCALS')





@contextmanager

def default\_socket\_timeout(timeout):

&#x20;   """Context temporarily setting the default socket timeout."""

&#x20;   prev = socket.getdefaulttimeout()

&#x20;   socket.setdefaulttimeout(timeout)

&#x20;   yield

&#x20;   socket.setdefaulttimeout(prev)





class bgThread(threading.Thread):

&#x20;   """Background service thread."""



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self, name=None, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;       super().\_\_init\_\_()

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_shutdown = threading.Event()

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_stopped = threading.Event()

&#x20;       self.daemon = True

&#x20;       self.name = name or self.\_\_class\_\_.\_\_name\_\_



&#x20;   def body(self):

&#x20;       raise NotImplementedError()



&#x20;   def on\_crash(self, msg, \*fmt, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;       print(msg.format(\*fmt), file=sys.stderr)

&#x20;       traceback.print\_exc(None, sys.stderr)



&#x20;   def run(self):

&#x20;       body = self.body

&#x20;       shutdown\_set = self.\_\_is\_shutdown.is\_set

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           while not shutdown\_set():

&#x20;               try:

&#x20;                   body()

&#x20;               except Exception as exc:  # pylint: disable=broad-except

&#x20;                   try:

&#x20;                       self.on\_crash('{0!r} crashed: {1!r}', self.name, exc)

&#x20;                       self.\_set\_stopped()

&#x20;                   finally:

&#x20;                       sys.stderr.flush()

&#x20;                       os.\_exit(1)  # exiting by normal means won't work

&#x20;       finally:

&#x20;           self.\_set\_stopped()



&#x20;   def \_set\_stopped(self):

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           self.\_\_is\_stopped.set()

&#x20;       except TypeError:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;           # we lost the race at interpreter shutdown,

&#x20;           # so gc collected built-in modules.

&#x20;           pass



&#x20;   def stop(self):

&#x20;       """Graceful shutdown."""

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_shutdown.set()

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_stopped.wait()

&#x20;       if self.is\_alive():

&#x20;           self.join(THREAD\_TIMEOUT\_MAX)





def release\_local(local):

&#x20;   """Release the contents of the local for the current context.



&#x20;   This makes it possible to use locals without a manager.



&#x20;   With this function one can release :class:`Local` objects as well as

&#x20;   :class:`StackLocal` objects.  However it's not possible to

&#x20;   release data held by proxies that way, one always has to retain

&#x20;   a reference to the underlying local object in order to be able

&#x20;   to release it.



&#x20;   Example:

&#x20;       >>> loc = Local()

&#x20;       >>> loc.foo = 42

&#x20;       >>> release\_local(loc)

&#x20;       >>> hasattr(loc, 'foo')

&#x20;       False

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   local.\_\_release\_local\_\_()





class Local:

&#x20;   """Local object."""



&#x20;   \_\_slots\_\_ = ('\_\_storage\_\_', '\_\_ident\_func\_\_')



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self):

&#x20;       object.\_\_setattr\_\_(self, '\_\_storage\_\_', {})

&#x20;       object.\_\_setattr\_\_(self, '\_\_ident\_func\_\_', get\_ident)



&#x20;   def \_\_iter\_\_(self):

&#x20;       return iter(self.\_\_storage\_\_.items())



&#x20;   def \_\_call\_\_(self, proxy):

&#x20;       """Create a proxy for a name."""

&#x20;       return Proxy(self, proxy)



&#x20;   def \_\_release\_local\_\_(self):

&#x20;       self.\_\_storage\_\_.pop(self.\_\_ident\_func\_\_(), None)



&#x20;   def \_\_getattr\_\_(self, name):

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           return self.\_\_storage\_\_\[self.\_\_ident\_func\_\_()]\[name]

&#x20;       except KeyError:

&#x20;           raise AttributeError(name)



&#x20;   def \_\_setattr\_\_(self, name, value):

&#x20;       ident = self.\_\_ident\_func\_\_()

&#x20;       storage = self.\_\_storage\_\_

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           storage\[ident]\[name] = value

&#x20;       except KeyError:

&#x20;           storage\[ident] = {name: value}



&#x20;   def \_\_delattr\_\_(self, name):

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           del self.\_\_storage\_\_\[self.\_\_ident\_func\_\_()]\[name]

&#x20;       except KeyError:

&#x20;           raise AttributeError(name)





class \_LocalStack:

&#x20;   """Local stack.



&#x20;   This class works similar to a :class:`Local` but keeps a stack

&#x20;   of objects instead.  This is best explained with an example::



&#x20;       >>> ls = LocalStack()

&#x20;       >>> ls.push(42)

&#x20;       >>> ls.top

&#x20;       42

&#x20;       >>> ls.push(23)

&#x20;       >>> ls.top

&#x20;       23

&#x20;       >>> ls.pop()

&#x20;       23

&#x20;       >>> ls.top

&#x20;       42



&#x20;   They can be force released by using a :class:`LocalManager` or with

&#x20;   the :func:`release\_local` function but the correct way is to pop the

&#x20;   item from the stack after using.  When the stack is empty it will

&#x20;   no longer be bound to the current context (and as such released).



&#x20;   By calling the stack without arguments it will return a proxy that

&#x20;   resolves to the topmost item on the stack.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self):

&#x20;       self.\_local = Local()



&#x20;   def \_\_release\_local\_\_(self):

&#x20;       self.\_local.\_\_release\_local\_\_()



&#x20;   def \_get\_\_ident\_func\_\_(self):

&#x20;       return self.\_local.\_\_ident\_func\_\_



&#x20;   def \_set\_\_ident\_func\_\_(self, value):

&#x20;       object.\_\_setattr\_\_(self.\_local, '\_\_ident\_func\_\_', value)

&#x20;   \_\_ident\_func\_\_ = property(\_get\_\_ident\_func\_\_, \_set\_\_ident\_func\_\_)

&#x20;   del \_get\_\_ident\_func\_\_, \_set\_\_ident\_func\_\_



&#x20;   def \_\_call\_\_(self):

&#x20;       def \_lookup():

&#x20;           rv = self.top

&#x20;           if rv is None:

&#x20;               raise RuntimeError('object unbound')

&#x20;           return rv

&#x20;       return Proxy(\_lookup)



&#x20;   def push(self, obj):

&#x20;       """Push a new item to the stack."""

&#x20;       rv = getattr(self.\_local, 'stack', None)

&#x20;       if rv is None:

&#x20;           # pylint: disable=assigning-non-slot

&#x20;           # This attribute is defined now.

&#x20;           self.\_local.stack = rv = \[]

&#x20;       rv.append(obj)

&#x20;       return rv



&#x20;   def pop(self):

&#x20;       """Remove the topmost item from the stack.



&#x20;       Note:

&#x20;           Will return the old value or `None` if the stack was already empty.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       stack = getattr(self.\_local, 'stack', None)

&#x20;       if stack is None:

&#x20;           return None

&#x20;       elif len(stack) == 1:

&#x20;           release\_local(self.\_local)

&#x20;           return stack\[-1]

&#x20;       else:

&#x20;           return stack.pop()



&#x20;   def \_\_len\_\_(self):

&#x20;       stack = getattr(self.\_local, 'stack', None)

&#x20;       return len(stack) if stack else 0



&#x20;   @property

&#x20;   def stack(self):

&#x20;       # get\_current\_worker\_task uses this to find

&#x20;       # the original task that was executed by the worker.

&#x20;       stack = getattr(self.\_local, 'stack', None)

&#x20;       if stack is not None:

&#x20;           return stack

&#x20;       return \[]



&#x20;   @property

&#x20;   def top(self):

&#x20;       """The topmost item on the stack.



&#x20;       Note:

&#x20;           If the stack is empty, :const:`None` is returned.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           return self.\_local.stack\[-1]

&#x20;       except (AttributeError, IndexError):

&#x20;           return None





class LocalManager:

&#x20;   """Local objects cannot manage themselves.



&#x20;   For that you need a local manager.

&#x20;   You can pass a local manager multiple locals or add them

&#x20;   later by appending them to ``manager.locals``.  Every time the manager

&#x20;   cleans up, it will clean up all the data left in the locals for this

&#x20;   context.



&#x20;   The ``ident\_func`` parameter can be added to override the default ident

&#x20;   function for the wrapped locals.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self, locals=None, ident\_func=None):

&#x20;       if locals is None:

&#x20;           self.locals = \[]

&#x20;       elif isinstance(locals, Local):

&#x20;           self.locals = \[locals]

&#x20;       else:

&#x20;           self.locals = list(locals)

&#x20;       if ident\_func is not None:

&#x20;           self.ident\_func = ident\_func

&#x20;           for local in self.locals:

&#x20;               object.\_\_setattr\_\_(local, '\_\_ident\_func\_\_', ident\_func)

&#x20;       else:

&#x20;           self.ident\_func = get\_ident



&#x20;   def get\_ident(self):

&#x20;       """Return context identifier.



&#x20;       This is the identifier the local objects use internally

&#x20;       for this context.  You cannot override this method to change the

&#x20;       behavior but use it to link other context local objects (such as

&#x20;       SQLAlchemy's scoped sessions) to the Werkzeug locals.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       return self.ident\_func()



&#x20;   def cleanup(self):

&#x20;       """Manually clean up the data in the locals for this context.



&#x20;       Call this at the end of the request or use ``make\_middleware()``.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       for local in self.locals:

&#x20;           release\_local(local)



&#x20;   def \_\_repr\_\_(self):

&#x20;       return '<{} storages: {}>'.format(

&#x20;           self.\_\_class\_\_.\_\_name\_\_, len(self.locals))





class \_FastLocalStack(threading.local):



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self):

&#x20;       self.stack = \[]

&#x20;       self.push = self.stack.append

&#x20;       self.pop = self.stack.pop

&#x20;       super().\_\_init\_\_()



&#x20;   @property

&#x20;   def top(self):

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           return self.stack\[-1]

&#x20;       except (AttributeError, IndexError):

&#x20;           return None



&#x20;   def \_\_len\_\_(self):

&#x20;       return len(self.stack)





if USE\_FAST\_LOCALS:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;   LocalStack = \_FastLocalStack

else:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;   # - See #706

&#x20;   # since each thread has its own greenlet we can just use those as

&#x20;   # identifiers for the context.  If greenlets aren't available we

&#x20;   # fall back to the  current thread ident.

&#x20;   LocalStack = \_LocalStack



```

"""Utilities related to dates, times, intervals, and timezones."""

from \_\_future\_\_ import annotations



import numbers

import os

from dateutil.parser import isoparse

from kombu.utils.functional import reprcall

from kombu.utils.objects import cached\_property



from .functional import dictfilter

from .text import pluralize

else:

&#x20;   from backports.zoneinfo import ZoneInfo





\_\_all\_\_ = (

&#x20;   'LocalTimezone', 'timezone', 'maybe\_timedelta',

&#x20;   'delta\_resolution', 'remaining', 'rate', 'weekday',

&#x20;   'humanize\_seconds', 'maybe\_iso8601', 'is\_naive',

&#x20;   'get\_exponential\_backoff\_interval',

)



C\_REMDEBUG = os.environ.get('C\_REMDEBUG', False)



DAYNAMES = 'sun', 'mon', 'tue', 'wed', 'thu', 'fri', 'sat'

WEEKDAYS = dict(zip(DAYNAMES, range(7)))



MONTHNAMES = 'jan', 'feb', 'mar', 'apr', 'may', 'jun', 'jul', 'aug', 'sep', 'oct', 'nov', 'dec'

YEARMONTHS = dict(zip(MONTHNAMES, range(1, 13)))



RATE\_MODIFIER\_MAP = {

&#x20;   's': lambda n: n,

&#x20;   'm': lambda n: n / 60.0,

&#x20;   'h': lambda n: n / 60.0 / 60.0,

}



TIME\_UNITS = (

&#x20;   ('day', 60 \* 60 \* 24.0, lambda n: format(n, '.2f')),

&#x20;   ('hour', 60 \* 60.0, lambda n: format(n, '.2f')),

&#x20;   ('minute', 60.0, lambda n: format(n, '.2f')),

&#x20;   ('second', 1.0, lambda n: format(n, '.2f')),

)



ZERO = timedelta(0)



\_local\_timezone = None





class LocalTimezone(tzinfo):

&#x20;   """Local time implementation. Provided in \_Zone to the app when `enable\_utc` is disabled.

&#x20;   Otherwise, \_Zone provides a UTC ZoneInfo instance as the timezone implementation for the application.



&#x20;   Note:

&#x20;       Used only when the :setting:`enable\_utc` setting is disabled.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   \_offset\_cache: dict\[int, tzinfo] = {}



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self) -> None:

&#x20;       # This code is moved in \_\_init\_\_ to execute it as late as possible

&#x20;       # See get\_default\_timezone().

&#x20;       self.STDOFFSET = timedelta(seconds=-\_time.timezone)

&#x20;       if \_time.daylight:

&#x20;           self.DSTOFFSET = timedelta(seconds=-\_time.altzone)

&#x20;       else:

&#x20;           self.DSTOFFSET = self.STDOFFSET

&#x20;       self.DSTDIFF = self.DSTOFFSET - self.STDOFFSET

&#x20;       super().\_\_init\_\_()



&#x20;   def \_\_repr\_\_(self) -> str:

&#x20;       return f'<LocalTimezone: UTC{int(self.DSTOFFSET.total\_seconds() / 3600):+03d}>'



&#x20;   def utcoffset(self, dt: datetime) -> timedelta:

&#x20;       return self.DSTOFFSET if self.\_isdst(dt) else self.STDOFFSET



&#x20;   def dst(self, dt: datetime) -> timedelta:

&#x20;       return self.DSTDIFF if self.\_isdst(dt) else ZERO



&#x20;   def tzname(self, dt: datetime) -> str:

&#x20;       return \_time.tzname\[self.\_isdst(dt)]



&#x20;   def fromutc(self, dt: datetime) -> datetime:

&#x20;       # The base tzinfo class no longer implements a DST

&#x20;       # offset aware .fromutc() in Python 3 (Issue #2306).

&#x20;       offset = int(self.utcoffset(dt).seconds / 60.0)

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           tz = self.\_offset\_cache\[offset]

&#x20;       except KeyError:

&#x20;           tz = self.\_offset\_cache\[offset] = datetime\_timezone(

&#x20;               timedelta(minutes=offset))

&#x20;       return tz.fromutc(dt.replace(tzinfo=tz))



&#x20;   def \_isdst(self, dt: datetime) -> bool:

&#x20;       tt = (dt.year, dt.month, dt.day,

&#x20;             dt.hour, dt.minute, dt.second,

&#x20;             dt.weekday(), 0, 0)

&#x20;       stamp = \_time.mktime(tt)

&#x20;       tt = \_time.localtime(stamp)

&#x20;       return tt.tm\_isdst > 0





class \_Zone:

&#x20;   """Timezone class that provides the timezone for the application.

&#x20;   If `enable\_utc` is disabled, LocalTimezone is provided as the timezone provider through local().

&#x20;   Otherwise, this class provides a UTC ZoneInfo instance as the timezone provider for the application.



&#x20;   Additionally this class provides a few utility methods for converting datetimes.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   def tz\_or\_local(self, tzinfo: tzinfo | None = None) -> tzinfo:

&#x20;       """Return either our local timezone or the provided timezone."""



&#x20;       # pylint: disable=redefined-outer-name

&#x20;       if tzinfo is None:

&#x20;           return self.local

&#x20;       return self.get\_timezone(tzinfo)



&#x20;   def to\_local(self, dt: datetime, local=None, orig=None):

&#x20;       """Converts a datetime to the local timezone."""



&#x20;       if is\_naive(dt):

&#x20;           dt = make\_aware(dt, orig or self.utc)

&#x20;       return localize(dt, self.tz\_or\_local(local))



&#x20;   def to\_system(self, dt: datetime) -> datetime:

&#x20;       """Converts a datetime to the system timezone."""



&#x20;       # tz=None is a special case since Python 3.3, and will

&#x20;       # convert to the current local timezone (Issue #2306).

&#x20;       return dt.astimezone(tz=None)



&#x20;   def to\_local\_fallback(self, dt: datetime) -> datetime:

&#x20;       """Converts a datetime to the local timezone, or the system timezone."""

&#x20;       if is\_naive(dt):

&#x20;           return make\_aware(dt, self.local)

&#x20;       return localize(dt, self.local)



&#x20;   def get\_timezone(self, zone: str | tzinfo) -> tzinfo:

&#x20;       """Returns ZoneInfo timezone if the provided zone is a string, otherwise return the zone."""

&#x20;       if isinstance(zone, str):

&#x20;           return ZoneInfo(zone)

&#x20;       return zone



&#x20;   @cached\_property

&#x20;   def local(self) -> LocalTimezone:

&#x20;       """Return LocalTimezone instance for the application."""

&#x20;       return LocalTimezone()



&#x20;   @cached\_property

&#x20;   def utc(self) -> tzinfo:

&#x20;       """Return UTC timezone created with ZoneInfo."""

&#x20;       return self.get\_timezone('UTC')





timezone = \_Zone()





def maybe\_timedelta(delta: int) -> timedelta:

&#x20;   """Convert integer to timedelta, if argument is an integer."""

&#x20;   if isinstance(delta, numbers.Real):

&#x20;       return timedelta(seconds=delta)

&#x20;   return delta





def delta\_resolution(dt: datetime, delta: timedelta) -> datetime:

&#x20;   """Round a :class:`\~datetime.datetime` to the resolution of timedelta.



&#x20;   If the :class:`\~datetime.timedelta` is in days, the

&#x20;   :class:`\~datetime.datetime` will be rounded to the nearest days,

&#x20;   if the :class:`\~datetime.timedelta` is in hours the

&#x20;   :class:`\~datetime.datetime` will be rounded to the nearest hour,

&#x20;   and so on until seconds, which will just return the original

&#x20;   :class:`\~datetime.datetime`.

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   delta = max(delta.total\_seconds(), 0)



&#x20;   resolutions = ((3, lambda x: x / 86400),

&#x20;                  (4, lambda x: x / 3600),

&#x20;                  (5, lambda x: x / 60))



&#x20;   args = dt.year, dt.month, dt.day, dt.hour, dt.minute, dt.second

&#x20;   for res, predicate in resolutions:

&#x20;       if predicate(delta) >= 1.0:

&#x20;           return datetime(\*args\[:res], tzinfo=dt.tzinfo)

&#x20;   return dt





def remaining(

&#x20;       start: datetime, ends\_in: timedelta, now: Callable | None = None,

&#x20;       relative: bool = False) -> timedelta:

&#x20;   """Calculate the real remaining time for a start date and a timedelta.



&#x20;   For example, "how many seconds left for 30 seconds after start?"



&#x20;   Arguments:

&#x20;       start (\~datetime.datetime): Starting date.

&#x20;       ends\_in (\~datetime.timedelta): The end delta.

&#x20;       relative (bool): If enabled the end time will be calculated

&#x20;           using :func:`delta\_resolution` (i.e., rounded to the

&#x20;           resolution of `ends\_in`).

&#x20;       now (Callable): Function returning the current time and date.

&#x20;           Defaults to :func:`datetime.now(timezone.utc)`.



&#x20;   Returns:

&#x20;       \~datetime.timedelta: Remaining time.

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   now = now or datetime.now(datetime\_timezone.utc)

&#x20;   end\_date = start + ends\_in

&#x20;   if relative:

&#x20;       end\_date = delta\_resolution(end\_date, ends\_in).replace(microsecond=0)



&#x20;   # Using UTC to calculate real time difference.

&#x20;   # Python by default uses wall time in arithmetic between datetimes with

&#x20;   # equal non-UTC timezones.

&#x20;   now\_utc = now.astimezone(timezone.utc)

&#x20;   end\_date\_utc = end\_date.astimezone(timezone.utc)

&#x20;   ret = end\_date\_utc - now\_utc

&#x20;   if C\_REMDEBUG:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;       print(

&#x20;           'rem: NOW:{!r} NOW\_UTC:{!r} START:{!r} ENDS\_IN:{!r} '

&#x20;           'END\_DATE:{} END\_DATE\_UTC:{!r} REM:{}'.format(

&#x20;               now, now\_utc, start, ends\_in, end\_date, end\_date\_utc, ret)

&#x20;       )

&#x20;   return ret





def rate(r: str) -> float:

&#x20;   """Convert rate string (`"100/m"`, `"2/h"` or `"0.5/s"`) to seconds."""

&#x20;   if r:

&#x20;       if isinstance(r, str):

&#x20;           ops, \_, modifier = r.partition('/')

&#x20;           return RATE\_MODIFIER\_MAP\[modifier or 's'](float(ops)) or 0

&#x20;       return r or 0

&#x20;   return 0





def weekday(name: str) -> int:

&#x20;   """Return the position of a weekday: 0 - 7, where 0 is Sunday.



&#x20;   Example:

&#x20;       >>> weekday('sunday'), weekday('sun'), weekday('mon')

&#x20;       (0, 0, 1)

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   abbreviation = name\[0:3].lower()

&#x20;   try:

&#x20;       return WEEKDAYS\[abbreviation]

&#x20;   except KeyError:

&#x20;       # Show original day name in exception, instead of abbr.

&#x20;       raise KeyError(name)





def yearmonth(name: str) -> int:

&#x20;   """Return the position of a month: 1 - 12, where 1 is January.



&#x20;   Example:

&#x20;       >>> yearmonth('january'), yearmonth('jan'), yearmonth('may')

&#x20;       (1, 1, 5)

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   abbreviation = name\[0:3].lower()

&#x20;   try:

&#x20;       return YEARMONTHS\[abbreviation]

&#x20;   except KeyError:

&#x20;       # Show original day name in exception, instead of abbr.

&#x20;       raise KeyError(name)





def humanize\_seconds(

&#x20;       secs: int, prefix: str = '', sep: str = '', now: str = 'now',

&#x20;       microseconds: bool = False) -> str:

&#x20;   """Show seconds in human form.



&#x20;   For example, 60 becomes "1 minute", and 7200 becomes "2 hours".



&#x20;   Arguments:

&#x20;       prefix (str): can be used to add a preposition to the output

&#x20;           (e.g., 'in' will give 'in 1 second', but add nothing to 'now').

&#x20;       now (str): Literal 'now'.

&#x20;       microseconds (bool): Include microseconds.

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   secs = float(format(float(secs), '.2f'))

&#x20;   for unit, divider, formatter in TIME\_UNITS:

&#x20;       if secs >= divider:

&#x20;           w = secs / float(divider)

&#x20;           return '{}{}{} {}'.format(prefix, sep, formatter(w),

&#x20;                                     pluralize(w, unit))

&#x20;   if microseconds and secs > 0.0:

&#x20;       return '{prefix}{sep}{0:.2f} seconds'.format(

&#x20;           secs, sep=sep, prefix=prefix)

&#x20;   return now





def maybe\_iso8601(dt: datetime | str | None) -> None | datetime:

&#x20;   """Either ``datetime | str -> datetime`` or ``None -> None``."""

&#x20;   if not dt:

&#x20;       return

&#x20;   if isinstance(dt, datetime):

&#x20;       return dt

&#x20;   return isoparse(dt)





def is\_naive(dt: datetime) -> bool:

&#x20;   """Return True if :class:`\~datetime.datetime` is naive, meaning it doesn't have timezone info set."""

&#x20;   return dt.tzinfo is None or dt.tzinfo.utcoffset(dt) is None





def \_can\_detect\_ambiguous(tz: tzinfo) -> bool:

&#x20;   """Helper function to determine if a timezone can detect ambiguous times using dateutil."""



&#x20;   return isinstance(tz, ZoneInfo) or hasattr(tz, "is\_ambiguous")





def \_is\_ambiguous(dt: datetime, tz: tzinfo) -> bool:

&#x20;   """Helper function to determine if a timezone is ambiguous using python's dateutil module.



&#x20;   Returns False if the timezone cannot detect ambiguity, or if there is no ambiguity, otherwise True.



&#x20;   In order to detect ambiguous datetimes, the timezone must be built using ZoneInfo, or have an is\_ambiguous

&#x20;   method. Previously, pytz timezones would throw an AmbiguousTimeError if the localized dt was ambiguous,

&#x20;   but now we need to specifically check for ambiguity with dateutil, as pytz is deprecated.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   return \_can\_detect\_ambiguous(tz) and dateutil\_tz.datetime\_ambiguous(dt)





def make\_aware(dt: datetime, tz: tzinfo) -> datetime:

&#x20;   """Set timezone for a :class:`\~datetime.datetime` object."""



&#x20;   dt = dt.replace(tzinfo=tz)

&#x20;   if \_is\_ambiguous(dt, tz):

&#x20;       dt = min(dt.replace(fold=0), dt.replace(fold=1))

&#x20;   return dt





def localize(dt: datetime, tz: tzinfo) -> datetime:

&#x20;   """Convert aware :class:`\~datetime.datetime` to another timezone.



&#x20;   Using a ZoneInfo timezone will give the most flexibility in terms of ambiguous DST handling.

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   if is\_naive(dt):  # Ensure timezone aware datetime

&#x20;       dt = make\_aware(dt, tz)

&#x20;   if dt.tzinfo == ZoneInfo("UTC"):

&#x20;       dt = dt.astimezone(tz)  # Always safe to call astimezone on utc zones

&#x20;   return dt





def to\_utc(dt: datetime) -> datetime:

&#x20;   """Convert naive :class:`\~datetime.datetime` to UTC."""

&#x20;   return make\_aware(dt, timezone.utc)





def maybe\_make\_aware(dt: datetime, tz: tzinfo | None = None,

&#x20;                    naive\_as\_utc: bool = True) -> datetime:

&#x20;   """Convert dt to aware datetime, do nothing if dt is already aware."""

&#x20;   if is\_naive(dt):

&#x20;       if naive\_as\_utc:

&#x20;           dt = to\_utc(dt)

&#x20;       return localize(

&#x20;           dt, timezone.utc if tz is None else timezone.tz\_or\_local(tz),

&#x20;       )

&#x20;   return dt





class ffwd:

&#x20;   """Version of ``dateutil.relativedelta`` that only supports addition."""



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self, year=None, month=None, weeks=0, weekday=None, day=None,

&#x20;                hour=None, minute=None, second=None, microsecond=None,

&#x20;                \*\*kwargs: Any):

&#x20;       # pylint: disable=redefined-outer-name

&#x20;       # weekday is also a function in outer scope.

&#x20;       self.year = year

&#x20;       self.month = month

&#x20;       self.weeks = weeks

&#x20;       self.weekday = weekday

&#x20;       self.day = day

&#x20;       self.hour = hour

&#x20;       self.minute = minute

&#x20;       self.second = second

&#x20;       self.microsecond = microsecond

&#x20;       self.days = weeks \* 7

&#x20;       self.\_has\_time = self.hour is not None or self.minute is not None



&#x20;   def \_\_repr\_\_(self) -> str:

&#x20;       return reprcall('ffwd', (), self.\_fields(weeks=self.weeks,

&#x20;                                                weekday=self.weekday))



&#x20;   def \_\_radd\_\_(self, other: Any) -> timedelta:

&#x20;       if not isinstance(other, date):

&#x20;           return NotImplemented

&#x20;       year = self.year or other.year

&#x20;       month = self.month or other.month

&#x20;       day = min(monthrange(year, month)\[1], self.day or other.day)

&#x20;       ret = other.replace(\*\*dict(dictfilter(self.\_fields()),

&#x20;                                  year=year, month=month, day=day))

&#x20;       if self.weekday is not None:

&#x20;           ret += timedelta(days=(7 - ret.weekday() + self.weekday) % 7)

&#x20;       return ret + timedelta(days=self.days)



&#x20;   def \_fields(self, \*\*extra: Any) -> dict\[str, Any]:

&#x20;       return dictfilter({

&#x20;           'year': self.year, 'month': self.month, 'day': self.day,

&#x20;           'hour': self.hour, 'minute': self.minute,

&#x20;           'second': self.second, 'microsecond': self.microsecond,

&#x20;       }, \*\*extra)





def utcoffset(

&#x20;       time: ModuleType = \_time,

&#x20;       localtime: Callable\[..., \_time.struct\_time] = \_time.localtime) -> float:

&#x20;   """Return the current offset to UTC in hours."""

&#x20;   if localtime().tm\_isdst:

&#x20;       return time.altzone // 3600

&#x20;   return time.timezone // 3600





def adjust\_timestamp(ts: float, offset: int,

&#x20;                    here: Callable\[..., float] = utcoffset) -> float:

&#x20;   """Adjust timestamp based on provided utcoffset."""

&#x20;   return ts - (offset - here()) \* 3600





def get\_exponential\_backoff\_interval(

&#x20;   factor: int,

&#x20;   retries: int,

&#x20;   maximum: int,

&#x20;   full\_jitter: bool = False

) -> int:

&#x20;   """Calculate the exponential backoff wait time."""

&#x20;   # Will be zero if factor equals 0

&#x20;   countdown = min(maximum, factor \* (2 \*\* retries))

&#x20;   # Full jitter according to

&#x20;   # https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/exponential-backoff-and-jitter/

&#x20;   if full\_jitter:

&#x20;       countdown = random.randrange(countdown + 1)

&#x20;   # Adjust according to maximum wait time and account for negative values.

&#x20;   return max(0, countdown)



```



\### 代码文件: celery\\utils\\timer2.py

```python

"""Scheduler for Python functions.



.. note::

&#x20;   This is used for the thread-based worker only,

&#x20;   not for amqp/redis/sqs/qpid where :mod:`kombu.asynchronous.timer` is used.

"""

import os

import sys

import threading

from itertools import count

from threading import TIMEOUT\_MAX as THREAD\_TIMEOUT\_MAX

from time import sleep

from typing import Any, Callable, Iterator, Optional, Tuple



from kombu.asynchronous.timer import Entry

from kombu.asynchronous.timer import Timer as Schedule

from kombu.asynchronous.timer import logger, to\_timestamp



TIMER\_DEBUG = os.environ.get('TIMER\_DEBUG')



\_\_all\_\_ = ('Entry', 'Schedule', 'Timer', 'to\_timestamp')





class Timer(threading.Thread):

&#x20;   """Timer thread.



&#x20;   Note:

&#x20;       This is only used for transports not supporting AsyncIO.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   Entry = Entry

&#x20;   Schedule = Schedule



&#x20;   running: bool = False

&#x20;   on\_tick: Optional\[Callable\[\[float], None]] = None



&#x20;   \_timer\_count: count = count(1)



&#x20;   if TIMER\_DEBUG:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;       def start(self, \*args: Any, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> None:

&#x20;           import traceback

&#x20;           print('- Timer starting')

&#x20;           traceback.print\_stack()

&#x20;           super().start(\*args, \*\*kwargs)



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self, schedule: Optional\[Schedule] = None,

&#x20;                on\_error: Optional\[Callable\[\[Exception], None]] = None,

&#x20;                on\_tick: Optional\[Callable\[\[float], None]] = None,

&#x20;                on\_start: Optional\[Callable\[\['Timer'], None]] = None,

&#x20;                max\_interval: Optional\[float] = None, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> None:

&#x20;       self.schedule = schedule or self.Schedule(on\_error=on\_error,

&#x20;                                                 max\_interval=max\_interval)

&#x20;       self.on\_start = on\_start

&#x20;       self.on\_tick = on\_tick or self.on\_tick

&#x20;       super().\_\_init\_\_()

&#x20;       # `\_is\_stopped` is likely to be an attribute on `Thread` objects so we

&#x20;       # double underscore these names to avoid shadowing anything and

&#x20;       # potentially getting confused by the superclass turning these into

&#x20;       # something other than an `Event` instance (e.g. a `bool`)

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_shutdown = threading.Event()

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_stopped = threading.Event()

&#x20;       self.mutex = threading.Lock()

&#x20;       self.not\_empty = threading.Condition(self.mutex)

&#x20;       self.daemon = True

&#x20;       self.name = f'Timer-{next(self.\_timer\_count)}'



&#x20;   def \_next\_entry(self) -> Optional\[float]:

&#x20;       with self.not\_empty:

&#x20;           delay: Optional\[float]

&#x20;           entry: Optional\[Entry]

&#x20;           delay, entry = next(self.scheduler)

&#x20;           if entry is None:

&#x20;               if delay is None:

&#x20;                   self.not\_empty.wait(1.0)

&#x20;               return delay

&#x20;       return self.schedule.apply\_entry(entry)

&#x20;   \_\_next\_\_ = next = \_next\_entry  # for 2to3



&#x20;   def run(self) -> None:

&#x20;       try:

&#x20;           self.running = True

&#x20;           self.scheduler: Iterator\[Tuple\[Optional\[float], Optional\[Entry]]] = iter(self.schedule)



&#x20;           while not self.\_\_is\_shutdown.is\_set():

&#x20;               delay = self.\_next\_entry()

&#x20;               if delay:

&#x20;                   if self.on\_tick:

&#x20;                       self.on\_tick(delay)

&#x20;                   if sleep is None:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;                       break

&#x20;                   sleep(delay)

&#x20;           try:

&#x20;               self.\_\_is\_stopped.set()

&#x20;           except TypeError:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;               # we lost the race at interpreter shutdown,

&#x20;               # so gc collected built-in modules.

&#x20;               pass

&#x20;       except Exception as exc:

&#x20;           logger.error('Thread Timer crashed: %r', exc, exc\_info=True)

&#x20;           sys.stderr.flush()

&#x20;           os.\_exit(1)



&#x20;   def stop(self) -> None:

&#x20;       self.\_\_is\_shutdown.set()

&#x20;       if self.running:

&#x20;           self.\_\_is\_stopped.wait()

&#x20;           self.join(THREAD\_TIMEOUT\_MAX)

&#x20;           self.running = False



&#x20;   def ensure\_started(self) -> None:

&#x20;       if not self.running and not self.is\_alive():

&#x20;           if self.on\_start:

&#x20;               self.on\_start(self)

&#x20;           self.start()



&#x20;   def \_do\_enter(self, meth: str, \*args: Any, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> Entry:

&#x20;       self.ensure\_started()

&#x20;       with self.mutex:

&#x20;           entry = getattr(self.schedule, meth)(\*args, \*\*kwargs)

&#x20;           self.not\_empty.notify()

&#x20;           return entry



&#x20;   def enter(self, entry: Entry, eta: float, priority: Optional\[int] = None) -> Entry:

&#x20;       return self.\_do\_enter('enter\_at', entry, eta, priority=priority)



&#x20;   def call\_at(self, \*args: Any, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> Entry:

&#x20;       return self.\_do\_enter('call\_at', \*args, \*\*kwargs)



&#x20;   def enter\_after(self, \*args: Any, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> Entry:

&#x20;       return self.\_do\_enter('enter\_after', \*args, \*\*kwargs)



&#x20;   def call\_after(self, \*args: Any, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> Entry:

&#x20;       return self.\_do\_enter('call\_after', \*args, \*\*kwargs)



&#x20;   def call\_repeatedly(self, \*args: Any, \*\*kwargs: Any) -> Entry:

&#x20;       return self.\_do\_enter('call\_repeatedly', \*args, \*\*kwargs)



&#x20;   def exit\_after(self, secs: float, priority: int = 10) -> None:

&#x20;       self.call\_after(secs, sys.exit, priority)



&#x20;   def cancel(self, tref: Entry) -> None:

&#x20;       tref.cancel()



&#x20;   def clear(self) -> None:

&#x20;       self.schedule.clear()



&#x20;   def empty(self) -> bool:

&#x20;       return not len(self)



&#x20;   def \_\_len\_\_(self) -> int:

&#x20;       return len(self.schedule)



&#x20;   def \_\_bool\_\_(self) -> bool:

&#x20;       """``bool(timer)``."""

&#x20;       return True

&#x20;   \_\_nonzero\_\_ = \_\_bool\_\_



&#x20;   @property

&#x20;   def queue(self) -> list:

&#x20;       return self.schedule.queue



```

A. HISTORY OF THE SOFTWARE

==========================



Python was created in the early 1990s by Guido van Rossum at Stichting

Mathematisch Centrum (CWI, see http://www.cwi.nl) in the Netherlands

as a successor of a language called ABC.  Guido remains Python's

principal author, although it includes many contributions from others.



In 1995, Guido continued his work on Python at the Corporation for

National Research Initiatives (CNRI, see http://www.cnri.reston.va.us)

in Reston, Virginia where he released several versions of the

software.



In May 2000, Guido and the Python core development team moved to

BeOpen.com to form the BeOpen PythonLabs team.  In October of the same

year, the PythonLabs team moved to Digital Creations (now Zope

Corporation, see http://www.zope.com).  In 2001, the Python Software

Foundation (PSF, see http://www.python.org/psf/) was formed, a

non-profit organization created specifically to own Python-related

Intellectual Property.  Zope Corporation is a sponsoring member of

the PSF.



All Python releases are Open Source (see http://www.opensource.org for

the Open Source Definition).  Historically, most, but not all, Python

releases have also been GPL-compatible; the table below summarizes

```python

"""Implementation of the Observer pattern."""

import sys

import threading

import warnings

import weakref

from weakref import WeakMethod



from kombu.utils.functional import retry\_over\_time



from celery.exceptions import CDeprecationWarning

from celery.local import PromiseProxy, Proxy

from celery.utils.functional import fun\_accepts\_kwargs

from celery.utils.log import get\_logger

from celery.utils.time import humanize\_seconds



\_\_all\_\_ = ('Signal',)



logger = get\_logger(\_\_name\_\_)





def \_make\_id(target):  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;   if isinstance(target, Proxy):

&#x20;       target = target.\_get\_current\_object()

&#x20;   if isinstance(target, (bytes, str)):

&#x20;       # see Issue #2475

&#x20;       return target

&#x20;   if hasattr(target, '\_\_func\_\_'):

&#x20;       return id(target.\_\_func\_\_)

&#x20;   return id(target)





def \_boundmethod\_safe\_weakref(obj):

&#x20;   """Get weakref constructor appropriate for `obj`.  `obj` may be a bound method.



&#x20;   Bound method objects must be special-cased because they're usually garbage

&#x20;   collected immediately, even if the instance they're bound to persists.



&#x20;   Returns:

&#x20;       a (weakref constructor, main object) tuple. `weakref constructor` is

&#x20;       either :class:`weakref.ref` or :class:`weakref.WeakMethod`.  `main

&#x20;       object` is the instance that `obj` is bound to if it is a bound method;

&#x20;       otherwise `main object` is simply `obj.

&#x20;   """

&#x20;   try:

&#x20;       obj.\_\_func\_\_

&#x20;       obj.\_\_self\_\_

&#x20;       # Bound method

&#x20;       return WeakMethod, obj.\_\_self\_\_

&#x20;   except AttributeError:

&#x20;       # Not a bound method

&#x20;       return weakref.ref, obj





def \_make\_lookup\_key(receiver, sender, dispatch\_uid):

&#x20;   if dispatch\_uid:

&#x20;       return (dispatch\_uid, \_make\_id(sender))

&#x20;   # Issue #9119 - retry-wrapped functions use the underlying function for dispatch\_uid

&#x20;   elif hasattr(receiver, '\_dispatch\_uid'):

&#x20;       return (receiver.\_dispatch\_uid, \_make\_id(sender))

&#x20;   else:

&#x20;       return (\_make\_id(receiver), \_make\_id(sender))





NONE\_ID = \_make\_id(None)



NO\_RECEIVERS = object()



RECEIVER\_RETRY\_ERROR = """\\

Could not process signal receiver %(receiver)s. Retrying %(when)s...\\

"""





class Signal:  # pragma: no cover

&#x20;   """Create new signal.



&#x20;   Keyword Arguments:

&#x20;       providing\_args (List): A list of the arguments this signal can pass

&#x20;           along in a :meth:`send` call.

&#x20;       use\_caching (bool): Enable receiver cache.

&#x20;       name (str): Name of signal, used for debugging purposes.

&#x20;   """



&#x20;   #: Holds a dictionary of

&#x20;   #: ``{receiverkey (id): weakref(receiver)}`` mappings.

&#x20;   receivers = None



&#x20;   def \_\_init\_\_(self, providing\_args=None, use\_caching=False, name=None):

&#x20;       self.receivers = \[]

&#x20;       self.providing\_args = set(

&#x20;           providing\_args if providing\_args is not None else \[])

&#x20;       self.lock = threading.Lock()

&#x20;       self.use\_caching = use\_caching

&#x20;       self.name = name

&#x20;       # For convenience we create empty caches even if they are not used.

&#x20;       # A note about caching: if use\_caching is defined, then for each

&#x20;       # distinct sender we cache the receivers that sender has in

&#x20;       # 'sender\_receivers\_cache'.  The cache is cleaned when .connect() or

&#x20;       # .disconnect() is called and populated on .send().

&#x20;       self.sender\_receivers\_cache = (

&#x20;           weakref.WeakKeyDictionary() if use\_caching else {}

&#x20;       )

&#x20;       self.\_dead\_receivers = False



&#x20;   def \_connect\_proxy(self, fun, sender, weak, dispatch\_uid):

&#x20;       return self.connect(

&#x20;           fun, sender=sender.\_get\_current\_object(),

&#x20;           weak=weak, dispatch\_uid=dispatch\_uid,

&#x20;       )



&#x20;   def connect(self, \*args, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;       """Connect receiver to sender for signal.



&#x20;       Arguments:

&#x20;           receiver (Callable): A function or an instance method which is to

&#x20;               receive signals.  Receivers must be hashable objects.



&#x20;               if weak is :const:`True`, then receiver must be

&#x20;               weak-referenceable.



&#x20;               Receivers must be able to accept keyword arguments.



&#x20;               If receivers have a `dispatch\_uid` attribute, the receiver will

&#x20;               not be added if another receiver already exists with that

&#x20;               `dispatch\_uid`.



&#x20;           sender (Any): The sender to which the receiver should respond.

&#x20;               Must either be a Python object, or :const:`None` to

&#x20;               receive events from any sender.



&#x20;           weak (bool): Whether to use weak references to the receiver.

&#x20;               By default, the module will attempt to use weak references to

&#x20;               the receiver objects.  If this parameter is false, then strong

&#x20;               references will be used.



&#x20;           dispatch\_uid (Hashable): An identifier used to uniquely identify a

&#x20;               particular instance of a receiver.  This will usually be a

&#x20;               string, though it may be anything hashable.



&#x20;           retry (bool): If the signal receiver raises an exception

&#x20;               (e.g. ConnectionError), the receiver will be retried until it

&#x20;               runs successfully. A strong ref to the receiver will be stored

&#x20;               and the `weak` option will be ignored.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       def \_handle\_options(sender=None, weak=True, dispatch\_uid=None,

&#x20;                           retry=False):



&#x20;           def \_connect\_signal(fun):



&#x20;               options = {'dispatch\_uid': dispatch\_uid,

&#x20;                          'weak': weak}



&#x20;               def \_retry\_receiver(retry\_fun):



&#x20;                   def \_try\_receiver\_over\_time(\*args, \*\*kwargs):

&#x20;                       def on\_error(exc, intervals, retries):

&#x20;                           interval = next(intervals)

&#x20;                           err\_msg = RECEIVER\_RETRY\_ERROR % \\

&#x20;                               {'receiver': retry\_fun,

&#x20;                                'when': humanize\_seconds(interval, 'in', ' ')}

&#x20;                           logger.error(err\_msg)

&#x20;                           return interval



&#x20;                       return retry\_over\_time(retry\_fun, Exception, args,

&#x20;                                              kwargs, on\_error)



&#x20;                   return \_try\_receiver\_over\_time



&#x20;               if retry:

&#x20;                   options\['weak'] = False

&#x20;                   if not dispatch\_uid:

&#x20;                       # if there's no dispatch\_uid then we need to set the

&#x20;                       # dispatch uid to the original func id so we can look

&#x20;                       # it up later with the original func id

&#x20;                       options\['dispatch\_uid'] = \_make\_id(fun)

&#x20;                   fun = \_retry\_receiver(fun)

&#x20;                   fun.\_dispatch\_uid = options\['dispatch\_uid']



&#x20;               self.\_connect\_signal(fun, sender, options\['weak'],

&#x20;                                    options\['dispatch\_uid'])

&#x20;               return fun



&#x20;           return \_connect\_signal



&#x20;       if args and callable(args\[0]):

&#x20;           return \_handle\_options(\*args\[1:], \*\*kwargs)(args\[0])

&#x20;       return \_handle\_options(\*args, \*\*kwargs)



&#x20;   def \_connect\_signal(self, receiver, sender, weak, dispatch\_uid):

&#x20;       assert callable(receiver), 'Signal receivers must be callable'

&#x20;       if not fun\_accepts\_kwargs(receiver):

&#x20;           raise ValueError(

&#x20;               'Signal receiver must accept keyword arguments.')



&#x20;       if isinstance(sender, PromiseProxy):

&#x20;           sender.\_\_then\_\_(

&#x20;               self.\_connect\_proxy, receiver, sender, weak, dispatch\_uid,

&#x20;           )

&#x20;           return receiver



&#x20;       lookup\_key = \_make\_lookup\_key(receiver, sender, dispatch\_uid)



&#x20;       if weak:

&#x20;           ref, receiver\_object = \_boundmethod\_safe\_weakref(receiver)

&#x20;           receiver = ref(receiver)

&#x20;           weakref.finalize(receiver\_object, self.\_remove\_receiver)



&#x20;       with self.lock:

&#x20;           self.\_clear\_dead\_receivers()

&#x20;           for r\_key, \_ in self.receivers:

&#x20;               if r\_key == lookup\_key:

&#x20;                   break

&#x20;           else:

&#x20;               self.receivers.append((lookup\_key, receiver))

&#x20;           self.sender\_receivers\_cache.clear()



&#x20;       return receiver



&#x20;   def disconnect(self, receiver=None, sender=None, weak=None,

&#x20;                  dispatch\_uid=None):

&#x20;       """Disconnect receiver from sender for signal.



&#x20;       If weak references are used, disconnect needn't be called.

&#x20;       The receiver will be removed from dispatch automatically.



&#x20;       Arguments:

&#x20;           receiver (Callable): The registered receiver to disconnect.

&#x20;               May be none if `dispatch\_uid` is specified.



&#x20;           sender (Any): The registered sender to disconnect.



&#x20;           weak (bool): The weakref state to disconnect.



&#x20;           dispatch\_uid (Hashable): The unique identifier of the receiver

&#x20;               to disconnect.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       if weak is not None:

&#x20;           warnings.warn(

&#x20;               'Passing `weak` to disconnect has no effect.',

&#x20;               CDeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)



&#x20;       lookup\_key = \_make\_lookup\_key(receiver, sender, dispatch\_uid)



&#x20;       disconnected = False

&#x20;       with self.lock:

&#x20;           self.\_clear\_dead\_receivers()

&#x20;           for index in range(len(self.receivers)):

&#x20;               (r\_key, \_) = self.receivers\[index]

&#x20;               if r\_key == lookup\_key:

&#x20;                   disconnected = True

&#x20;                   del self.receivers\[index]

&#x20;                   break

&#x20;           self.sender\_receivers\_cache.clear()

&#x20;       return disconnected



&#x20;   def has\_listeners(self, sender=None):

&#x20;       return bool(self.\_live\_receivers(sender))



&#x20;   def send(self, sender, \*\*named):

&#x20;       """Send signal from sender to all connected receivers.



&#x20;       If any receiver raises an error, the exception is returned as the

&#x20;       corresponding response. (This is different from the "send" in

&#x20;       Django signals. In Celery "send" and "send\_robust" do the same thing.)



&#x20;       Arguments:

&#x20;           sender (Any): The sender of the signal.

&#x20;               Either a specific object or :const:`None`.

&#x20;           \*\*named (Any): Named arguments which will be passed to receivers.



&#x20;       Returns:

&#x20;           List: of tuple pairs: `\[(receiver, response), … ]`.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       responses = \[]

&#x20;       if not self.receivers or \\

&#x20;               self.sender\_receivers\_cache.get(sender) is NO\_RECEIVERS:

&#x20;           return responses



&#x20;       for receiver in self.\_live\_receivers(sender):

&#x20;           try:

&#x20;               response = receiver(signal=self, sender=sender, \*\*named)

&#x20;           except Exception as exc:  # pylint: disable=broad-except

&#x20;               if not hasattr(exc, '\_\_traceback\_\_'):

&#x20;                   exc.\_\_traceback\_\_ = sys.exc\_info()\[2]

&#x20;               logger.exception(

&#x20;                   'Signal handler %r raised: %r', receiver, exc)

&#x20;               responses.append((receiver, exc))

&#x20;           else:

&#x20;               responses.append((receiver, response))

&#x20;       return responses

&#x20;   send\_robust = send  # Compat with Django interface.



&#x20;   def \_clear\_dead\_receivers(self):

&#x20;       # Warning: caller is assumed to hold self.lock

&#x20;       if self.\_dead\_receivers:

&#x20;           self.\_dead\_receivers = False

&#x20;           new\_receivers = \[]

&#x20;           for r in self.receivers:

&#x20;               if isinstance(r\[1], weakref.ReferenceType) and r\[1]() is None:

&#x20;                   continue

&#x20;               new\_receivers.append(r)

&#x20;           self.receivers = new\_receivers



&#x20;   def \_live\_receivers(self, sender):

&#x20;       """Filter sequence of receivers to get resolved, live receivers.



&#x20;       This checks for weak references and resolves them, then returning only

&#x20;       live receivers.

&#x20;       """

&#x20;       receivers = None

&#x20;       if self.use\_caching and not self.\_dead\_receivers:

&#x20;           receivers = self.sender\_receivers\_cache.get(sender)

&#x20;           # We could end up here with NO\_RECEIVERS even if we do check this

&#x20;           # case in .send() prior to calling \_Live\_receivers()  due to

&#x20;           # concurrent .send() call.

&#x20;           if receivers is NO\_RECEIVERS:

&#x20;               return \[]

&#x20;       if receivers is None:

&#x20;           with self.lock:

&#x20;               self.\_clear\_dead\_receivers()

&#x20;               senderkey = \_make\_id(sender)

&#x20;               receivers = \[]

&#x20;               for (receiverkey, r\_senderkey), receiver in self.receivers:

&#x20;                   if r\_senderkey == NONE\_ID or r\_senderkey == senderkey:

&#x20;                       receivers.append(receiver)

&#x20;               if self.use\_caching:

&#x20;                   if not receivers:

&#x20;                       self.sender\_receivers\_cache\[sender] = NO\_RECEIVERS

&#x20;                   else:

&#x20;                       # Note: we must cache the weakref versions.

&#x20;                       self.sender\_receivers\_cache\[sender] = receivers

&#x20;       non\_weak\_receivers = \[]

&#x20;       for receiver in receivers:

&#x20;           if isinstance(receiver, weakref.ReferenceType):

&#x20;               # Dereference the weak reference.

&#x20;               receiver = receiver()

&#x20;               if receiver is not None:

&#x20;                   non\_weak\_receivers.append(receiver)

&#x20;           else:

&#x20;               non\_weak\_receivers.append(receiver)

&#x20;       return non\_weak\_receivers



&#x20;   def \_remove\_receiver(self, receiver=None):

&#x20;       """Remove dead receivers from connections."""

&#x20;       # Mark that the self..receivers first has dead weakrefs. If so,

&#x20;       # we will clean those up in connect, disconnect and \_live\_receivers

&#x20;       # while holding self.lock.  Note that doing the cleanup here isn't a

&#x20;       # good idea, \_remove\_receiver() will be called as a side effect of

&#x20;       # garbage collection, and so the call can happen wh ile we are already

&#x20;       # holding self.lock.

&#x20;       self.\_dead\_receivers = True



&#x20;   def \_\_repr\_\_(self):

&#x20;       """``repr(signal)``."""

&#x20;       return f'<{type(self).\_\_name\_\_}: {self.name} providing\_args={self.providing\_args!r}>'



&#x20;   def \_\_str\_\_(self):

&#x20;       """``str(signal)``."""

&#x20;       return repr(self)



```

\_\_all\_\_ = ('Signal',)

"""Static files."""

import os





def get\_file(\*args):

&#x20;   # type: (\*str) -> str

&#x20;   """Get filename for static file."""

&#x20;   return os.path.join(os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(\_\_file\_\_)), \*args)





def logo():

&#x20;   # type: () -> bytes

&#x20;   """Celery logo image."""

&#x20;   return get\_file('celery\_128.png')
